S&P Global Inc. (SPGI) — Deep-Dive Dossier
Run date: 2026-07-01 · FY2025 ended Dec 31, 2025 · No valuation / no buy-sell view by design

S&P Global Inc. (NYSE: SPGI) — Deep-dive dossier

Contents
1. Business description, model & unit economics 2. Key technologies & technical differentiators 3. Competitive advantages & moat assessment 4. Competitive landscape & market sizing 5. Run date 2026-07-01 (written 2026-07-02). Fiscal year = calendar year; FY2025 = year ended 2025-12-31 (10-K filed 2026-02-11). Latest reported period: Q1 2026 (10-Q filed 2026-04-28).

Capitalization (Bloomberg, pulled 2026-07-01)

Item Value
Shares outstanding 303.2M (BBG basis; 10-Q cover count 296.0M at 2026-04-24)
Price $414.97 (2026-07-01 intraday, post-spin)
Market capitalization $125,819M
Net debt $11,508M (3/31/2026: total debt $13,318M − cash $1,810M)
Enterprise value $142,941M (BBG)

Consensus multiples (BEst, pulled 2026-07-01)

Multiple (consensus, BEst) FY2026E FY2027E FY2028E
Forward P/E (adjusted EPS) 21.1x 18.7x 16.7x
EV / EBIT 17.1x 15.7x 14.4x
EV / EBITDA 16.8x 15.4x 14.2x
Dividend yield (indicated $3.88) 0.9% — —

Fiscal year end December 31. Pull date 2026-07-01, Bloomberg BEst consensus. EV derivation: market cap plus net debt gives $137,327M; the BBG EV of $142,941M additionally includes the $4,917M redeemable noncontrolling interest in S&P Dow Jones Indices and other NCI. Basis warning: the Mobility spin-off completed the morning of 2026-07-01 and BEst estimates were not yet recast — every forward multiple above divides a post-spin price by Mobility-inclusive estimates. On the clean like-for-like basis (2026-06-30 close of $407.26, cum-Mobility, over the same estimates) the forward P/E is 20.7x / 18.3x / 16.4x. Consensus BEst DPS beyond the indicated rate was not pulled. Descriptive market context only; no valuation judgment.

Run basis

Item Value
Run date 2026-07-01
Reporting entity S&P Global Inc. (CIK 64040), consolidated, five reportable segments through FY2025
Fiscal basis Calendar year; FY2025 ended 2025-12-31
Latest data cut 10-Q Q1 2026 (filed 2026-04-28); Q1 2026 call 2026-04-28; BBG pull 2026-07-01
Source refresh Archive: 10 10-Ks (FY2016-FY2025), 6 10-Qs, 24 earnings + 186 presentation transcripts, 15 decks (878 slides); live Bloomberg terminal
Scope This dossier contains no valuation judgment, no price target, and no buy/sell view

Global caveats

Issue Reading note
Mobility-inclusive basis expires The Mobility spin (NYSE: MBGL, 1-for-1) completed 2026-07-01, the run date. Every consolidated figure, the FY2026 guide, and all visible CY2026-28 consensus still include Mobility (11.4% of FY2025 revenue, ~40% adjusted EBITDA margin). SPGI will recast 2025 quarters ex-Mobility and re-guide in H2 2026; every level-based table and comparison in this dossier is on a basis that expires with that recast.
GAAP and adjusted diverge by up to ~14pp The 2022 IHS Markit merger put ~$1.1B/yr of acquisition-intangible amortization into GAAP results (MI alone $588M in 2025). MI runs 20.2% GAAP vs 34.0% adjusted; Mobility 21.6% GAAP vs ~40% adjusted. The two bases are never mixed in one comparison here.
Indices is 73%-owned S&P Dow Jones Indices is consolidated at 100% but CME/CGIS own 27% (redeemable NCI $4,917M with a put right). The quoted 68.7% segment margin is not SPGI's economics; net of NCI the margin is 51.3%, and $322M of FY2025 segment profit accrued to CME/CGIS rather than to SPGI shareholders.
The bear case is reconstructed, not sourced No SPGI-specific short thesis exists in any local store; the one sell-side document (Goldman Sachs, 2026-04-26) is a rebuttal of the AI-disruption bear case. The bear framing in §8 and §18 is assembled from price action, the company's own risk-factor changes, and call Q&A — a real short thesis may contain arguments not covered.

Billed issuance dollars are disclosed only from 2022, so earlier cycles are analyzed on revenue proxies. Ratings segment revenue includes a $172M (2025) intersegment royalty from Market Intelligence, so segment and external revenue differ by ~$175M.

1. Business description, model & unit economics

S&P Global sells benchmarks, data, and workflow software into three end-markets: capital markets (Ratings, Indices, Market Intelligence), energy and commodities (Energy, formerly Commodity Insights), and automotive (Mobility, spun off 2026-07-01). FY2025 revenue was $15,336M across five segments (10-K FY2025, Note 12):

Segment FY2025 rev ($M) % of total FY23 gr FY24 gr FY25 gr
Market Intelligence 4,916 32.0% 15% 6% 6%
Ratings 4,724 29.7% 9% 31% 8%
Energy 2,299 15.0% 15% 10% 7%
Mobility 1,747 11.4% 30% 8% 9%
Indices 1,850 12.0% 5% 16% 14%
Intersegment elim. (200) — — — —
Total 15,336 100% 12% 14% 8%

Ratings' 29.7% is on an external-revenue basis (before the $172M royalty MI pays to resell Ratings content); the $4,724M is total segment revenue. The FY23–FY25 columns are YoY growth of gross segment revenue; MI, Energy, and Mobility FY23 growth is lifted by the IHS Markit merger anniversary (closed February 2022). Roughly 79% of revenue is recurring (subscription, surveillance, asset-linked, recurring variable); the other ~21% is transaction revenue geared to debt-issuance volumes. The market-geared share did most of the recent work: of the $2,839M revenue increase from 2023 to 2025, 53% came from the ~31% of revenue that is issuance-, AUM-, or volume-linked (10-K FY2025 revenue-type table).▪ The binding constraint on the whole company is demand-side customer activity — issuance volumes, ETF AUM, trading volumes, data budgets — with no supply-side capacity limit anywhere in the filings.

The demand-driver tree — The "~79% recurring" figure above understates the cyclicality, because the recurring bucket itself contains ~$1.2B of equity-market-beta asset-linked fees (falling ~6%/yr) plus issuance spillover, so the truly market-insensitive annuity is closer to two-thirds of revenue and about half of profit. Weighted by profit rather than revenue, the company reduces to three exposures, not one secular annuity: the global debt-issuance cycle (Ratings transaction), the equity-market level net of ~6%/yr fee compression (Indices asset-linked), and a real subscription annuity (Ratings surveillance plus MI and Energy data).

Segment / driver Share of revenue / profit (FY2025) Ultimate exposure (decomposed) Realized 3-yr contribution (2023→2025) Robustness
Ratings 30% rev / 46% profit Not 'the ratings franchise': 52% is transaction = global debt-issuance $ volume × realized bps, a rate/credit-cycle bet (billed issuance $2,539B→$4,327B, +70%); 48% is a surveillance annuity, ~5.5%/yr, each rated bond paying to maturity ~48% of group revenue growth; within Ratings, ~75% (+$1,045M) was the cyclical transaction line, ~25% (+$347M) the annuity; organic, no merger Half structural-cyclical (txn −45% in 2022, billed +54% in 2024), half structural-recurring; highest amplitude in the company
Indices 12% rev / 19% profit (~14% net of the 27% CME NCI) Not 'passive growth / GDP+': asset-linked (65% of segment) = equity-market LEVEL (ETF AUM) × a realized fee falling ~6%/yr (3.73→2.53 bps since 2018); ETD royalties (18%) = a long-volatility hedge on SPX volumes ~16% of group growth; asset-linked +$347M while average AUM rose +65% (fees captured only ~half of AUM growth); market beta, not unit growth Structural franchise, but revenue is market beta minus fee compression; ETD is counter-cyclical (+38% in the down-2022 market)
Market Intelligence 32% rev / 15% profit Enterprise data/software budgets of banks and asset managers (~56% of ACV is IB + investment management); ~6% organic ACV, price-vs-seat split undisclosed ~19% of group growth, organic ~6%/yr; but the 2022 reported step was the IHS Markit MERGER, and recent adds are partly purchased (Visible Alpha; With Intelligence $1.8B) Recurring/secular, low amplitude; the one franchise the compounder label fits, and the one carrying the AI-substitution tail
Energy 15% rev / 14% profit Commodity-market activity + energy-sector data budgets; the durable core is the Platts benchmark royalty (5% of segment, +16%/yr) ~12% of group growth; ~88% subscription, but the 2026 guide was cut twice (sanctions/Iran) and the volatility-geared lines (Global Trading Services, exchange royalties) produced the recent quarter's growth Mostly recurring subscription; a small high-quality royalty core (Platts benchmarks) attached to an ordinary vertical-data business
Mobility (spun 2026-07-01) 11% rev / 6% profit US used-car transaction volume via CARFAX (65% of segment) + auto OEM/supplier data budgets ~9% of group growth, recurring/organic Recurring, but REMOVED from the go-forward company by the 2026-07-01 spin

Confirmed against the realized decade (§13.3), the recurring-compounder narrative did not carry the results on its own: the 2022 EPS decline (−18% GAAP) and the 2024-25 recovery were the debt-issuance cycle (Ratings transaction −45%, then billed issuance +54%/+11%), stepped up once by the 2022 IHS Markit merger and levered by the equity-market level running Indices, while the smooth annuity (Ratings surveillance plus MI subscriptions) grew steadily underneath but is only about half of profit.

1.1 Ratings — the issuer-pays fee on the world's debt issuance

Debt issuers (corporations, financial institutions, sovereigns, structured-finance sponsors) pay for a new-issue rating at the point of sale and then pay annually for surveillance, relationship fees, and entity ratings. The revenue equation is: transaction revenue = billed issuance × realized fee rate, plus a non-transaction annuity (surveillance, annual fees, Ratings Evaluation Services, majority-owned Crisil, and the $172M royalty MI pays to resell Ratings content). FY2025 split: transaction $2,470M (52%), non-transaction $2,254M (48%) (10-K FY2025, Ratings MD&A).▪

The disclosed volume-price decomposition:

Year Transaction rev $M Billed issuance $B Realized rate (bps)
2022 1,241 2,338 5.31
2023 1,425 2,539 5.61
2024 2,326 3,911 5.95
2025 2,470 4,327 5.71
Q1 2026 712 1,230 (qtr) 5.79

Sources: 10-K FY2025 and FY2024 MD&A billed-issuance tables; 10-Q 2026-Q1. Three mechanisms set the rate:

  • List-price escalation: S&P's published US fee schedule lists 8.35 bps with a $155,000 minimum on most US corporate transactions as of January 2026, up 3.1% from 8.10 bps a year earlier▪ (morningstar.com, accessed 2026-07-01 [web, unverified — spglobal.com/usratingsfees and the Morningstar page both blocked automated fetch]); nine of the ten 10-Ks FY2016-FY2025 disclose realized price capture ("improved contract terms across product categories" or equivalent — every fiscal year except 2017).
  • Realized capture: roughly 69-71% of that list price [estimate: disclosed transaction revenue ÷ disclosed billed issuance vs the published list; rests on the unverified list figures], the wedge being frequent-issuer terms, jumbo-deal fee caps, and government/loan mix.
  • Mix: swings the rate by ±10% between periods — Q4 2025 billed issuance grew 28% while transaction revenue grew only 12% because flows shifted out of high-yield and bank loans into fee-capped investment grade (Q4 2025 call, CFO), and Q1 2026 repeated the pattern (IG billed issuance +41% on AI-hyperscaler and M&A debt, transaction revenue +15%).

The annuity half compounded 5.5%/yr over 2017-2025 with only shallow declines — down 0.3% in 2019 and 1.9% in 2022 (FX-driven; +1% constant currency) — against the transaction line's worst year of −45% (2022). Every rated new issue enters the surveillance base and pays annually until maturity; management calls this the flywheel (Investor Day 2025-11-13, CFO).

Unit economics (no BOM exists — the product is an opinion produced by salaried analysts, so the margin concept collapses into the operating margin):

Economic unit Units (FY2025) Realized price Cost per unit Reconciliation
$1B of billed issuance 4,327 ($B) $571K per $1B (5.71 bps) marginal cost ≈ analyst time; no per-issue cost disclosed 4,327 × 5.71 bps = $2,470M = disclosed transaction revenue
One outstanding rating 1,055,822 ratings (Dec 2023; 895,230 government) (SEC OCR Jan-2025 report, Chart 2) [web] core annuity ≈ $1,783M [estimate: non-txn 2,254 − 172 royalty − ~299 Crisil research] → ~$1,700 average, ~$11,100 per non-government rating (bounds) — 1,783 + 172 + 299 = $2,254M non-transaction
One credit analyst 1,725 analysts (Dec 2024, SEC OCR 2025 report, Chart 9) [web] revenue per analyst ≈ $2.6M segment expense per analyst ≤ ~$900K [estimate: upper bound, excludes Crisil staff] consistent with 63.8% GAAP segment margin
MI royalty contract 1 contract $172M (2025) ≈ nil ~100%-margin internal licensing, grows with MI Credit & Risk ACV

The cost base is almost entirely people. Expenses sat in a $1,343-1,471M band for seven years (2017-2023) while revenue swung between $2,883M and $4,097M; expense CAGR 2016-2025 was 3.3% against revenue CAGR of 7.2%. Realized incremental margins ran 68-105% in the up-years from 2019 (2017, an up-year distorted by legal-cost noise, ran 56%), with a symmetric 91% decremental in 2022 — the down-year removed $1,047M of revenue and $957M of operating profit, and margin declined 9.4pp in one year (10-K segment tables, FY2016-FY2025).▪ Incentive compensation is the shock absorber in both directions. Capital intensity is near zero: segment assets $1,137M against $4,724M revenue, capex $64M (1.4% of revenue), and fees are billed at issuance or annually in advance.

1.2 Market Intelligence — enterprise-priced data and workflow

MI sells subscription data and workflow across three lines — Data, Analytics & Insights (Capital IQ Pro desktop, feeds/APIs, Visible Alpha, With Intelligence); Enterprise Solutions (loan-market and private-markets workflow: Wall Street Office, ClearPar, iLevel); and Credit & Risk Solutions (resale of Ratings content via RatingsXpress/RatingsDirect) — with FY2025 revenue and segment share below (quarterly-update decks, sums tie to the 10-K segment total).▪

Line FY2025 ($M) % of segment
Data, Analytics & Insights 2,786 56.7%
Enterprise Solutions 1,238 25.2%
Credit & Risk Solutions 893 18.2%

Revenue type FY2025:

Revenue type FY2025 ($M) % of MI
Subscription 4,107 83%
Recurring variable 623 13%
Non-subscription — 4%

Recurring variable is fees per trade processed, AUM, and positions valued; the figures are Market Intelligence segment (subscription $4,107M ≈ 83% of the $4,916M segment). Customers: ~56% of the 2021 ACV book was investment banking plus investment management (Investor Day 2022 deck, slide 27) — the end-market whose 2023-24 budget cuts produced MI's cancellation episode.

Pricing is enterprise value-based, not per-seat: "we don't do the headcount model... we want a fair share of that value" (Investor Day 2025-11-13, MI president Saha).▪ Contracts are multi-year, invoiced annually in advance; consolidated unearned revenue was $4,088M at end-2025, and customers prefund roughly a quarter of the year (10-K FY2025). Disclosed price evidence: organic ACV growth ~6% for six straight quarters, net renewal rates up ~100bp y/y (level undisclosed), two clients paid 35-45% renewal increases for AI-ready data access, and one buy-side client displaced an incumbent at a ~20% premium (Q1 2026 call).▪ The price-vs-volume split inside ACV growth is undisclosed.

Unit economics: the billable unit is the enterprise contract, and the CODM footnote states sub-segment expenses are not allocated, so no per-unit cost stack can be built. The two disclosed anchors: the average subscription relationship is roughly $50-140K/yr [estimate: two-angle triangulation — Cap IQ Pro procurement data (median ≈$53K/yr, range $15-215K, Vendr data via costbench.com, accessed 2026-07-01 [web, unverified]) against book-÷-customer bounds; low-medium confidence, scale anchor only], with a long right tail of multimillion-dollar enterprise deals; and C&RS pays Ratings a disclosed $172M content royalty, 19.3% of its $893M line — an ~81% content-acquisition gross margin before its own opex. Segment level: FY2025 adjusted expenses $3,245M on $4,916M revenue give a 34.0% adjusted operating margin; $588M of acquisition-intangible amortization takes GAAP to 20.2%. Realized incremental adjusted margins post-merger: 35.6% (2023), 25.3% (2024, the cancellation trough), 59.4% (2025), 38.9% cumulative 2022→2025 — modest fixed-cost leverage for a data business, with the surplus reinvested in AI features and absorbed by M&A dilution. Growth is partly purchased: With Intelligence cost $1,800M (closed 2025-11-25) at roughly 10-11x revenue [estimate: $1.8B ÷ annualized ~$165M].

1.3 Indices — a royalty on passive assets and derivatives volumes

S&P Dow Jones Indices licenses index IP three ways (FY2025) — asset-linked fees (bp-of-AUM fees from ETF and mutual-fund sponsors), exchange-traded-derivatives royalties (CME pays a profit share on its equity-index complex, $193M in 2025; the ~$131M residual is mostly Cboe SPX options, terms undisclosed), and data subscriptions — with FY2025 revenue and segment share below.▪

Stream FY2025 ($M) % of segment
Asset-linked fees 1,206 65%
ETD royalties 324 18%
Data subscriptions 320 17%

SPGI owns 73%; CME/CGIS hold 27% as a redeemable NCI.

Asset-linked fees convert AUM into revenue at a declining rate. The blended proxy rate (asset-linked fees ÷ average ETF AUM — the numerator includes mutual-fund and OTC fees, so the level overstates the pure ETF rate; the trend is the signal [estimate: computed from disclosed series]) declined from ~4.4bp (2016) and 3.85bp (2020) to 2.53bp (2025) and 2.43bp annualized in Q1 2026. That is a compound decline of ~6%/yr over nine years, though not a monotonic one: the rate rose in 2019 and again in 2022.▪ The mechanism is disclosed: flows concentrate in the lowest-priced S&P 500 core products, especially in volatile markets (Q1 2026 call, CFO). Over 2021-2025 asset-linked revenue captured only ~52% of average-AUM growth. Contract-level pricing is widely dispersed. SPY pays 3.0bp of daily trust size plus $600K/yr under a license running to 2031 — $186M in its September-2025 fiscal year, ~15.4% of SPGI's asset-linked line on ~13% of the AUM (SPY N-30D filed 2025-11-25, archived from EDGAR) — while the rest of the ETF book realizes roughly 1.5-2.0bp [estimate: residual after SPY; VOO's 3bp total expense ratio is a hard ceiling on its license fee].

The ETD royalty is the volatility hedge: $125M (2016) → $324M (2025), +18% in Q1 2026 on record SPX volumes, and +38% in 2022 when equities declined 19% — the segment grew revenue 17% in the worst equity year of the decade.▪ Per-contract arithmetic: CME's $193M equals ~$0.10 per equity-index contract traded, or 16.5% of CME's equity-index transaction fees [estimate: two consistent angles from CME's disclosed ADV and fee lines].

Unit economics: marginal cost of an incremental tracked dollar or traded contract is ~zero; the cost base is a discretionary opex pool ($579M, 31% of revenue — compensation, technology, partner royalties). The nine-year incremental margin (70.8%) barely exceeds the in-place margin (68.7%), because the JV re-spends as it grows; margin rose only 4.0pp over nine years, and 2023 showed the negative case (asset-linked fees flat on +8% AUM, total revenue +5%, profit flat, margin −3.3pp). Every marginal profit dollar loses ~25% to the CME/CGIS interest: FY2025 operating profit $1,271M less $322M NCI leaves $949M, a 51.3% net margin.▪

1.4 Energy and Mobility — two vertical-data franchises (Mobility spun 2026-07-01)

Energy (fka Commodity Insights): $2,299M FY2025 = subscription $2,016M (88%, enterprise rate-card contracts of 50-100 products per client), Platts exchange royalties $120M (5% — licensing price assessments to commodity exchanges for cash settlement; 16.1% CAGR 2021-2025, +27% in Q1 2026), and non-subscription $163M (7%, CERAWeek and consulting; the Q1 conference season is worth roughly $70-90M [estimate: two angles — seasonal excess and attendance × realized price; CERAWeek list pricing is registration-gated]). Production cost is people, not compute: 450+ price reporters produce 15,000+ assessments daily from market-contributed data, so marginal COGS ≈ 0. GAAP margin 41.0% FY2025 (adjusted ≈47.5%), up from 35% in 2022 on 55% realized incremental adjusted margins.▪ Business-line dollars are undisclosed; the Upstream data line is bounded at $300-480M [estimate: ordinal-rank floor + SLB-deal materiality ceiling], grew under 1% in FY2025, declined 5% in Q1 2026, and is shedding its software quarter (~25% of Upstream revenue) to SLB. The most recent quarter cuts against the FY picture: Q1 2026 subscription growth halved to +4% (sanctions removed 100-140bp from the two core lines; the Iran conflict pressured Middle East clients), and half the quarter's growth came from the 12% of revenue that is usage- and event-driven.

Mobility (now Mobility Global, MBGL): $1,747M FY2025 = subscription $1,422M (81%) + non-subscription $325M. Two units: CARFAX, 65% of revenue (≈$1,142M carve-out basis) — 40,000+ dealer subscribers averaging roughly $2,000-2,150/dealer/month across the product suite [estimate: revenue share ÷ dealer count; two angles agree within ~1.5x], a consumer list price of $44.99/report, and a financial line used by 17 of the top-20 lenders; and B2B Solutions, 35% (>$600M) — forecasting for all top-40 OEMs and 94 of the top-100 suppliers, registration data covering >90% of US market volume, automotiveMastermind in 3,200 dealers. The data estate (38B records from 177,000 sources, ~6M records added daily) is acquired substantially by barter — service shops and police agencies contribute records in exchange for products and brand exposure — so marginal report cost is ~zero and the biggest cash cost is brand and selling ($1.5B cumulative media spend since 2000).▪ Standalone economics (Form-10 basis via the 2026-05-12 Investor Day): 40.6% adjusted EBITDA margin, flat for three years by choice ("growth comes first"); FCF conversion only 63-65% of adjusted EBITDA because the IHS merger left a $1.0B deferred tax liability costing $80-90M/yr of cash for ~12 years.▪ The standalone reset costs $20-25M of run-rate public-company expense plus an SBC step from ~1% to 3-4% of revenue — roughly 8-11% of 2025 adjusted EBITDA before the promised +50bp/yr expansion resumes.

2. Key technologies & technical differentiators

SPGI is not a product-technology company; its durable assets are benchmark standards with network effects (S&P 500, Platts, GICS, iBoxx, LoanX), curated datasets accumulated over decades (Compustat, SNL, CARFAX, Upstream well data), workflow software wrapped around those datasets, and an AI layer (Kensho, MCP integrations) whose job is to defend and re-price the first three as consumption shifts to LLM channels. Management quantified the mix at the 2025 Investor Day: over 95% of revenue from proprietary benchmarks, data and tools; benchmarks are the majority of revenue and over 70% of operating income▪; less than 5% of revenue is "undifferentiated." The same analysis at the MI level is less flattering: 12% of MI revenue is undifferentiated (~$590M [estimate: 12% × $4,902M]) and another 23% is redistributed Ratings content (Q1 2026 deck, slide 11)▪ — the attribution is management's own, published mid-de-rating, unaudited.

Capital IQ Pro. The MI desktop and delivery platform: 350,000 users, 6 trillion data points, AI features shipped (ChatIQ, Document Intelligence 2.0, Chart Explainer) with more than a third of users engaging them (Q1 2026 call). Management's own architecture argument concedes the UI layer is replicable — "it's possible that [desktop consumption] will go down" — while claiming the data and computation layers keep the value under MCP delivery (Investor Day 2025-11-13, Saha). The desktop is the most substitutable layer in the company: management sizes Cap IQ Pro at about 6% of enterprise revenue (Q4 2025 call), roughly $920M.▪

Kensho and the LLM-ready API. The in-house AI unit (acquired 2018, ~$550M; roughly $1B deployed against AI since 2018 — Q4 2025 call). Its industrial tools (Extract: 1B+ pages processed; Scribe: 1M+ audio hours; Link: 500M+ entities matched) are accumulated processing volumes rivals cannot copy quickly; the customer-facing LLM-ready API reached 300+ customers under contract or trial within ~3 quarters of launch, with API call volume up 5x quarter-over-quarter (Q1 2026 call).▪ Every adoption number is company-selected and unaudited, Kensho has no separately disclosed revenue after eight years, and its costs sit in Corporate Unallocated. The dependency risk is filed: SPGI's AI products rest on third-party models whose providers "may discontinue such models" (10-K FY2025, Item 1A — new language this year).

MCP integrations and the S&P Global Plug-in. S&P was a launch partner for Claude for Financial Services (July 2025) and now distributes licensed data into Claude, Microsoft Copilot, Google Gemini Enterprise, and OpenAI via MCP connectors, with no-training clauses and license gating ("we don't allow the LLM providers to train on S&P Global data" — Q4 2025 call).▪ Building an MCP server is not hard — FactSet, Moody's and Morningstar are also Claude connectors (claude.com/connectors, accessed 2026-07-01 [web]) — so the differentiation is the dataset, not the connector. The monetization model for third-party-channel consumption is explicitly still evolving; management prices at the enterprise level and says usage economics will follow.

Platts price assessments. 15,000+ daily assessments from 450+ reporters; ~70% of global waterborne crude trade is priced using Platts benchmarks (management claim, Investor Day 2025-11-13).▪ Benchmark status is a two-sided network reinforced by regulation (EU Benchmark Regulation authorization, IOSCO PRA alignment since 2012) and by exchange-settled derivatives ($120M royalties, +27% Q1 2026). The substitution precedent is real but bounded: Saudi Aramco moved US-bound crude pricing off Platts WTI to the Argus Sour Crude Index in 2009-10, with Kuwait and Iraq following (wikipedia.org, accessed 2026-07-01 [web]) — proof a benchmark that drifts from the physical market loses the contract, and also that only one leg moved.

Index IP. The S&P 500's position rests on ~70 years of history, benchmark-regulation authorization, and the SPX/E-mini derivatives liquidity network. The technology content is modest; the fee-driven switching precedent (Vanguard moved 22 funds, ~$537B, off MSCI to CRSP/FTSE in 2012; MSCI declined 27% in a day — etfstrategy.com, accessed 2026-07-01 [web]) has never touched a flagship S&P 500 fund in the 13 years since. Erosion instead shows up as realized-price mix-down (§1.3). Tokenized indices (iBoxx Treasuries on-chain, a tokenized S&P 500 with Centrifuge) are launched optionality with immaterial revenue.

GICS and LoanX identifiers. Low-revenue, high-switching-cost reference standards (GICS co-owned with MSCI; LoanX IDs the syndicated-loan identifier) that make S&P data the join-key for everyone else's data. Replacement requires the industry to re-map historical data simultaneously; no displacement attempt has traction.

Curated datasets. Compustat (1962 origin), SNL, CARFAX (38B records), Upstream well data (~80,000 wells, 30+ years) were built by hand-digitization and contributor relationships that cannot be re-run: "employees would have to physically scan microfiche... many of these types of data sets are only available in digital formats from S&P Global" (Q1 2026 call, CEO). Contributory datasets (Visible Alpha's 250+ broker models, With Intelligence, Platts submissions) add a network layer a scraper cannot copy. The filed counter-risk: "Third parties could also use our data with AI tools to create their own insights and potentially supplant our products and services" (10-K FY2025, Item 1A).

Enterprise workflow platforms. Wall Street Office ($4T+ AUM administered), ClearPar (the de facto syndicated-loan settlement utility), iLevel (16B+ private-markets data points). These are network utilities with regulatory overhead (DORA attestation), and they are currently the fastest-growing part of MI — Enterprise Solutions grew 14% organically in Q1 2026 — which cuts against the thesis that workflow software is the most AI-vulnerable layer.▪ The named collective threat is Versana, a consortium of 14 top-tier banks (including founding investors J.P. Morgan and Bank of America, the agent banks whose data feeds WSO/ClearPar) building a golden-source loan-data utility (versana.io, accessed 2026-07-01 [web]).

CERA Titan (hard launch late 2026) is the highest-roadmap-risk item: an unshipped AI-native platform is the stated fix for the one declining business line (Upstream), with management itself guiding "several quarters" before growth returns.

Infrastructure dependencies sit above all of this: AWS is the named critical cloud provider (a $1.0B five-year purchase obligation, ~$200M/yr), and third-party data suppliers "are also competitors" who have already raised fees (10-K FY2025, Item 1A).

3. Competitive advantages & moat assessment

The four franchises hold four different grades of advantage. Ratings and Indices are structural advantages of the first class — the reference class of franchises that support pricing power, throw off excess returns with almost no defensive reinvestment, and stay durable against well-funded attackers — each with specific, quantified qualifications.▪ Market Intelligence holds a real but contestable advantage: excess return in narrow niches, industry-average economics in aggregate, liable to compress. Energy and Mobility each contain one first-class core (Platts benchmarks; CARFAX) wrapped in ordinary vertical-data businesses, and the capital paid to assemble them earns below the cost of capital.

One gap qualifies the whole section: SPGI's AI positioning was examined in depth, but Bloomberg's, LSEG's, FactSet's and Moody's equivalent LLM-platform integrations were not, so the conclusion that MI's data layer survives AI re-platforming rests on one side's evidence (§20).

3.1 Ratings — structural at the core, contested at the frontier

The product is a de facto standard, not a service. Most investment guidelines and index-inclusion rules name Fitch, Moody's and S&P specifically — DBRS states that "with limited exception, all major indices exclude securities that do not have a credit rating from Fitch, Moody's, or S&P" (SEC OCR 2025 Staff Report, pp. 29-30, archived) — so an issuer of index-eligible public debt cannot substitute away at any price a challenger could offer.▪ NRSRO registration requires QIB certifications of three years' ratings use, a statutory chicken-and-egg the SEC's own report lists among persistent entry barriers. The financials are what that structure predicts: a 63.8% GAAP segment margin (FY2025), ~80% incremental margins across the decade, price capture disclosed in nine of ten years, and $4,724M of revenue produced on $1,137M of segment assets and $64M of capex.▪ The advantage passed the hardest available test: the structured-finance ratings failure of the financial crisis cost $1.6B in legal and regulatory settlements (charged 2014-2015) and did not dislodge share or pricing — S&P still held 49.6% of all NRSRO ratings outstanding at end-2024 and raised realized prices throughout.▪ A moat that survives its own worst case is durable against attackers, because the attackers' opening already came and went.

Two qualifications. First, the moat protects S&P's share of the fee pool, not the pool's size: transaction revenue is issuance-geared and the leverage cuts both ways (the 2022 down-cycle removed 9.4pp of margin in one year at a 91% decremental; the CFO concedes "the margin will be partly cycle dependent"). Second, the moat runs at full strength only where the two-rating norm binds. In structured finance, where one or two ratings suffice and five agencies compete deal-by-deal, S&P's US CLO share declined from 60.5% to 47.0% and CMBS from 38.1% to 13.0% over 2023-2025 (SEC OCR 2025 report, Green Street data); in private credit the index-inclusion mechanism does not operate, rating shopping is "more acute" per the SEC, and the volume leader in middle-market assessments is KBRA.▪ These are the industry's two fastest-growing issuance categories.

Management claims: largest provider of credit ratings in the world; analyst capacity as the invested core; competition on quality and speed of execution, "we don't compete on Ratings outcomes"; reputation as a key competitive strength (Investor Day 2025-11-13; 10-K FY2025 Item 1A). The 10-K has no Ratings competition section naming rivals; the regulatory-barrier description does the work.

External sources: the SEC's own report lists the entry barriers as persistent while documenting the frontier share losses; ESMA shows S&P's EU turnover share declining 0.8pp in one cycle (to 47.81%) with Fitch gaining [web]; Goldman Sachs (sector report, 2026-04-26) scores SPGI among the most structurally AI-resilient names — an AI model can estimate credit risk but cannot be an NRSRO, and no LLM vendor accepts accountability for a rating opinion. No external source disputes the core moat.

Ground-up view: a rating is a language, and languages tip to standards — a new entrant's AAA means nothing until the market has watched a cohort of its ratings season, which takes a full credit cycle and is what the QIB rule codifies. Minimum efficient scale is coverage breadth: surveillance across ~1.06M ratings requires an analyst base a challenger cannot fund at challenger fees — Morningstar DBRS, the largest agency outside the big three, runs a 32.4% adjusted margin at $0.7M revenue per analyst against the big two's low-60s margins at $2.5-2.6M per analyst. Switching costs sit with the instrument (a rated bond keeps its agency for life), making the annuity nearly unassailable. Buyer power is weak at the core and real where the share losses are.

One calibration point keeps this a half-step below the Visa/Mastercard single-company case: the advantage is the shared property of a duopoly. S&P holds no material economic edge over Moody's — adjusted margins 65% vs 63.6% in FY2025, a gap that narrowed from ~2.9pp in 2024; revenue per analyst only ~12% higher; the margin gap oscillating within ±3pp of parity across 2021-2025.▪ The big two run near-identical low-60s margins on the industry's two largest revenue bases and the largest agency outside the big three runs half that level; Fitch's profitability is undisclosed (Hearst is private), so the full industry profit split cannot be established. The durable excess return belongs to the two-player structure, not to S&P's execution within it.

Over time: stable to improving at the core — the take-rate rose 5.31→5.71bp over 2022-2025, the revenue lead over Moody's MIS widened from 1.06x (2020) to 1.10x (2025), and the segment margin recovered through a full cycle — with erosion confined to frontier categories and to consolidated GAAP presentation inherited from the merger.

3.2 Indices — the strongest position in the portfolio, with a falling fee rate and a 27% partner

The core asset is not index calculation (a commodity skill — CRSP proved an academic shop can supply Vanguard) but the S&P 500 liquidity ecosystem: $5.48T of linked ETF AUM at end-2025, an exclusive ownership-linked CME futures/options license that is effectively perpetual while CME holds its JV stake, and a benchmark name that is itself the product — an S&P 500 fund that switches index ceases to be an S&P 500 fund.▪ Thirteen years after Vanguard demonstrated the switching move against MSCI, no flagship S&P 500 product has defected, and the segment converts the position into a 68.7% GAAP margin with essentially no capital.▪

Two quantified caveats. First, the advantage produces quantity, not unit price: the realized asset-linked rate compounded down ~7.5%/yr over 2019-2025 (proxy 4.08bp → 2.53bp, 2.43bp annualized in Q1 2026), with one up-year in 2022, because concentrated buyers negotiate hard and flows concentrate in the cheapest tier.▪ Base growth has outrun the fee decline every year — over 2019-2025 the realized rate declined 38% cumulatively while asset-linked fees rose 97% and segment operating profit rose 102% — but the crossover risk materializes in flat markets: at the recent rate trend, AUM must grow ~7%/yr for asset-linked revenue to grow at all. Second, SPGI shareholders own 73% of this: the 68.7% consolidated margin is a 51.3% net margin after CME/CGIS's share, and the $4.9B redeemable put obligation grows as the JV does.

Management claims: largest index provider in the world by linked AUM; secular growth from market appreciation plus active-to-passive; a countercyclical ETD hedge (confirmed in 2022: segment revenue +17% in a down-equity year on ETD royalties +38%); a 10-12% medium-term growth target that embeds 5-7% annual market appreciation▪.

External sources: Burton-Taylor puts 2025 index-industry revenue at a record $7.2B (+13.4%) with S&P DJI #1 at $1.85B, MSCI $1.79B, FTSE Russell $1.29B (etfstream.com, accessed 2026-07-01 [web]); the Vanguard 2012 defection and LSEG's disclosed 2024 mandate loss show sponsors can move benchmarks where the brand is not consumer-facing; SPY's own filings show the index license is roughly a third of that fund's entire cost stack.

Ground-up view: entry fails at demand, not production — an index with no derivatives open interest, no benchmarked AUM, and no brand is a spreadsheet. The installed base is self-reinforcing (more AUM → tighter spreads and deeper ETD books → lower total cost of ownership → more AUM), and the CME structure fuses the largest distribution channel to the index owner: a strength for durability, a 27% tax on economics, and a $4.9B contingent call.

Over time: the volume moat is widening (AUM 3.2x over 2019-2025; ETD royalties $140M→$324M; data subscriptions at three straight double-digit quarters) faster than the price moat erodes; the margin gap vs MSCI's Index segment is stable, and the scale gap vs FTSE Russell widened in dollars.

3.3 Market Intelligence — real but contestable

The durable parts are narrower than the marketing. Three niches hold real advantage: mission-critical loan and private-markets workflow (WSO, ClearPar, iLevel — industry plumbing with multi-party network effects, +14% organic in Q1 2026); exclusive commercial distribution of S&P Ratings content (rides the upstream duopoly; +6%); and unreproducible historical archives (Compustat, SNL, point-in-time fundamentals).▪ Those support a ~96% recurring revenue mix and rising renewal rates.

But the financial evidence of a wide moat is missing. MI's organic growth has been pinned at ~6% for three years and Q1 2026 — the same rate as measured global financial-market-data spend (+6.5% in 2025, Burton-Taylor [web]) — so MI holds share rather than compounding it.▪ The adjusted margin was 34.1% in 2021 and 34.0% in 2025: zero net expansion across four post-merger years, with no premium over Moody's Analytics (33.1% adjusted FY2025, expanding faster at +240bp) or FactSet (32.2% GAAP FY2025), while MSCI operates ~20 points higher.▪ The two assets in old MI with the most obvious pricing power, CUSIP and LCD, were sold in 2022 as merger remedies. The evidence supports the middle case: some excess return today, defensible for now, liable to compress. The 2025-26 data points cut in MI's favor at the margin — renewal rates +100bp, AI-customer ACV growth ~30% above other customers, retention ~200bp higher among AI-feature users, two 35-45% AI renewal uplifts — but the aggregate has not moved: API call volume grew 5x quarter-over-quarter while ACV growth ticked down to ~6% from the 6.5-7% quoted over the prior two quarters.

Management claims: "deepest and most comprehensive proprietary data and tools in its market"; only 12% of MI revenue undifferentiated; enterprise value pricing insulating ACV from seat cuts. External sources: Goldman scores SPGI resilient while the market de-rated the whole sector ~30%; Burton-Taylor's market math shows MI at ~10% share of a Bloomberg-led industry, flat for three years. Ground-up view: content businesses reward the largest revenue base, and the desktop scale winner is Bloomberg (~$13-15B revenue, >355,000 terminals [web]); the excess-return niches available to a #3 are the workflow-utility, exclusive-content, and archive positions MI owns; the aggregated segment blends those with a contested desktop and a commodity tail, which is what the flat 34% margin and market-rate growth show.

Over time: mildly eroding in relative terms — Moody's Analytics is growing 9% vs MI's 6% and closing the scale gap from below; FactSet's organic ASV re-accelerated to +6.7% (Feb 2026), past MI's ~6% — offset by improving retention indicators. One quarterly flag on the closest pure-play comp: FactSet's GAAP operating margin declined ~650bp y/y to 26.7% in its May-2026 quarter on +6.4% revenue while MI expanded 80bp; FactSet's own disclosures do not identify the cause.

3.4 Energy & Mobility — first-class cores inside ordinary data businesses, below-hurdle acquisition returns

The two cores are structural. Platts: once an assessment is written into physical contracts and exchange-cleared derivatives, switching requires coordinated industry action, and IOSCO/BMR authorization prices out casual entry; the royalty toll doubled from $60M (2019) to $120M (2025) and grew 27% in Q1 2026. CARFAX: a contributory database that cannot be backfilled (history is the barrier), 36 OEM certified-pre-owned programs that will not certify a car without a CARFAX report, consumer pull of 2M "show me the CARFAX" requests a month, and realized consumer pricing of $44.99 per report against Experian AutoCheck's $29.99 — a ~1.5x premium sustained against the only substantive rival▪ (capitalone.com, accessed 2026-07-01 [web]; the page priced AutoCheck at $24.99 at first access and $29.99 on re-check).

The businesses around those cores are contestable. Upstream declined 5% in Q1 2026 and SPGI is selling its software portfolio to SLB — a retreat from a niche where Enverus (>$500M ARR, being acquired by Blackstone at >$6B [web]) out-scales S&P's ~$415-440M line [estimate]. Energy's blended margin (41.0% GAAP) still sits 6-13pp below what standalone Platts earned pre-merger (52-54%), because the IHS-heritage businesses earn less than the benchmark core. Mobility's Manufacturing line grew 1% in Q4 2025 on OEM budget tightening, and automotiveMastermind's 3,200 dealers are an eighth of CARFAX's footprint a decade in.

The returns test: both franchises are nearly capital-free at the operating level (capex 0.5%/1.6% of revenue) and both expanded margins every year since 2022 — but SPGI's balance sheet holds $8,543M of segment assets against Energy and $12,974M against Mobility, mostly merger goodwill, so returns on the capital actually paid run roughly 8.5% for Energy and 2.3-4.1% for Mobility [estimate: segment NOPAT ÷ segment assets at the 22.6% consolidated tax rate] against an 8-9% hurdle.▪ Mobility earns clearly below the cost of the capital paid to own it, Energy roughly at it — a judgment on the merger price, not the operating moats, and consistent with the portfolio now being taken apart (Mobility spun, Upstream software sold, EDM/thinkFolio and OSTTRA sold).

3.5 The returns evidence, three ways

ROIC = GAAP operating income × (1 − effective tax rate) ÷ average invested capital (total debt + total equity incl. NCI + redeemable NCI − cash and short-term investments). ROIIC = ΔNOPAT ÷ Δinvested capital. Cost-of-capital yardstick ≈ 8-9% [estimate: US large-cap WACC at beta ≈1.1]. Business-quality test only.

Standalone. Pre-merger SPGI ran 59-102% ROIC on a buyback-shrunken capital base (2016-2021). The all-stock IHS Markit merger reset invested capital from $3.1B to $49.4B; consolidated ROIC troughed at 6.5% (2023) and recovered to 10.6% (2025) — now just clearing the hurdle — as NOPAT compounds against a static goodwill block. The post-merger growth itself consumed no capital: NOPAT rose $1.3B from 2022 to 2025 while invested capital shrank $2.0B. Ratings on its own segment assets earns ~205% after tax [estimate; segment goodwill is allocated outside segment assets, so not comparable to consolidated peer ROICs]; the gap between that and the consolidated 10.6% is the merger price sitting in other segments' goodwill.

Vs peers (computed from each company's filed XBRL, pull 2026-07-01; GM uses each filer's cost-of-revenue tag — for SPGI/MCO a proxy, not true COGS; ICE is gross-revenue basis; MSCI/VRSK book equity is negative or near zero from buybacks, flattering their ROIC):

Company 5-yr rev CAGR FY20→25 FY25 GM FY25 op margin R&D int. Capex int. ROIC FY25 MRQ end MRQ rev y/y MRQ op margin
SPGI (consol.) 15.6% (merger-inflated; organic ~6-8%) 70.2% 42.2% n/d 1.3% 10.6% 2026-03-31 +10.4% 48.0% (≈43.8% ex-$175M disposal gains)
— Ratings segment 5.6% n/a 63.8% GAAP / 65% adj n/d 1.4% ~205% on segment assets [estimate] 2026-03-31 +13% 67.7% GAAP
— Indices segment 13.3% n/d 68.7% (51.3% net of NCI) n/d ~nil n/c (segment IC not disclosed) 2026-03-31 +16.6% 71.7% GAAP
Moody's (MCO) 7.5% 74.4% 43.4% n/d 4.2% 31.1% 2026-03-31 +8.1% 44.3%
MSCI 13.1% n/d 54.7% 5.7% 1.3% 44.5%* 2026-03-31 +14.1% 53.7%
FactSet (FDS) 9.2% 52.7% 32.2% 13.0% 4.7% 20.7% 2026-05-31 +6.4% 26.7%†
Verisk (VRSK) 6.2% 69.9% 43.7% 1.3% 7.9% 35.9%* 2026-03-31 +3.9% 45.0%
ICE (gross rev) 8.9% n/d 39.0% n/d 6.3% 7.7% 2026-03-31 +13.5% 45.4%
CME 6.0% n/d 64.9% n/d 1.3% 13.3% 2026-03-31 +14.5% 69.7%

* Flattered by near-zero or negative book equity. † FactSet's most recent quarter declined ~650bp y/y (33.2% → 26.7%); its own filings do not identify the cause. Five-year gap trends: SPGI's consolidated operating-margin premium over this set eroded (48.6% → 42.2%) — entirely merger amortization, since the Ratings and Indices segment margins rose over the same window — while MCO was roughly stable and MSCI/CME/VRSK/FDS margins rose. Only two business models in this quality peer set produce >60% segment margins (the two rating agencies and CME's exchange), and Ratings does it with the least capital of any of them.

Over time. The Ratings and Indices moats are stable-to-widening on their own financials; MI's relative position is mildly eroding; Energy/Mobility core tolls are widening while the data businesses around them erode. The consolidated ROIC trend (6.5% → 10.6% over 2023-2025) is amortization-and-growth arithmetic, not a competitive signal.

4. Competitive landscape & market sizing

4.1 Credit ratings

The industry is a big-three oligopoly at the core and a five-player market in non-government categories.▪ Shares of ratings outstanding at 2024-12-31: S&P 49.6%, Moody's 31.4%, Fitch 12.4%, DBRS 3.1%, KBRA 2.1% (SEC OCR 2025 Staff Report, Chart 5, archived; sec.gov, accessed 2026-07-01 [web]). HHI ≈ 3,614 on all ratings outstanding; ≈2,206 excluding government securities [estimate: sum of squared shares]. The large three earned 92.4% of aggregate NRSRO revenue in FY2024; erosion to DBRS and KBRA has averaged well under 0.5pp/yr since 2007 (big-three count share 98.8% then, 93.4% now). The industry fee pool is roughly $12B for FY2024 [estimate: revenue-share residual; Fitch's revenue triangulates to $2,400-3,200M, low confidence — Hearst is private and Rule 17g-3 reports are non-public].

Player Ratings revenue FY2025 5-yr CAGR Share outstanding (all / ex-gov) Analysts (2024) Rev/analyst Segment margin Share direction
S&P Global Ratings $4,549M external +5.5% 49.6% / 31.4% 1,725 $2.6M 63.8% GAAP / 65% adj Aggregate lead widening vs MCO; losing US CLO/CMBS share
Moody's MIS $4,119M external +4.6% 31.4% / 25.1% 1,673 $2.5M 63.6% adj Stable #2; EU share drifting down
Fitch (Hearst, private) ~$2,400-3,200M [estimate] n/d 12.4% / 20.1% 1,110 ~$2.2-2.9M [estimate] n/d; Hearst reports record 2025 profit attributed to Fitch [web] Gaining: EU +1.6pp; #1 US CLO (50.9%) and CMBS (68.0%) in 2025
Morningstar DBRS $354M +21.7% FY25 3.1% / 11.5% 517 $0.7M 32.4% adj Gaining in ABS/CMBS/Canada-EU corporates
KBRA (private) ~$300-400M [estimate] counts ~+18%/yr 2.1% / 6.1% 282 n/d n/d Fastest count growth; #1 US RMBS (49.1%); private-credit volume leader

The agencies set list price and issuers pay; the realized take-rate went 5.3bp (2022) → 5.7-5.8bp (2025/Q1 2026) with "improved contract terms" disclosed in 2024, 2025 and Q1 2026.▪ The contested arena is structured finance, where an agency is credited per deal and shares sum above 100%: S&P's US CLO share went 60.5% → 47.0% and CMBS 38.1% → 13.0% over 2023-2025 while Fitch took #1 in both; S&P gained in RMBS (17.2% → 29.4%) from a #4 position. Whether the CLO/CMBS retreat is deliberate criteria discipline or lost mandates is not disclosed by any party and no available source distinguishes them — the answer determines how much frontier weakness projects onto the core (§20). Counter-evidence, ranked:

  • Private credit: KBRA leads private-credit assessment volume (3,649 middle-market assessments in 2025 across >$1T of covered debt — kbra.com, accessed 2026-07-01 [web]).
  • Bank loans: bank-loan billed issuance declined high-teens in Q1 2026 as private credit and AI-disruption concerns shrink the broadly-syndicated market where S&P's coverage is strongest.
  • Revenue-pool erosion: DBRS and KBRA took ~1pp of the industry revenue pool (~$120M/yr) over three years.
  • Government sponsorship: governments can sponsor local competitors (a named 10-K risk).

In-sourcing is not a substitute for index-eligible ratings.

4.2 Index licensing

Global index-industry revenue was a record $7.2B in 2025 (+13.4%), with the top three holding over two-thirds: S&P DJI 25.7%, MSCI 24.9%, FTSE Russell 17.9%, Nasdaq 11.5%; HHI ≈ 1,750-1,850 [estimate] (Burton-Taylor via trade press [web]).▪ On the fee base itself S&P DJI's lead is much larger:

Provider Linked ETF/ETP AUM end-2025 5-yr AUM CAGR FY2025 index revenue Rev growth FY25 Segment margin Share direction
S&P DJI $5,480B 22.4% $1,850M +14% 68.7% GAAP (51.3% net of NCI) 5-yr share of ETF assets roughly held (~25→27.6% [estimate]); lost share of 2025 flows
MSCI Index $2,341B 16.2% $1,787M +11.9% 76.4% adj EBITDA Gaining at the margin: AUM +35.7% in 2025; $103B Q1 2026 inflows vs S&P DJI −2% q/q AUM
FTSE Russell (LSEG) $1,827B n/d (basis break) £954M ≈ $1,190M +7.3% organic 66.6% adj EBITDA Holding; disclosed a 2024 mandate loss [web]
Nasdaq Index $882B 19.7% $827M +17% n/d Fastest revenue growth; record $99B TTM inflows [web]
Bloomberg Index Services >$1,000B in FI ETFs; claims 55% of passive FI AUM [web] n/d n/d (private) n/d n/d The fixed-income incumbent; SPGI's iBoxx is the challenger
Morningstar + CRSP >$4,200B "linked assets" (broader basis) [web] n/d small n/d n/d Feb-2026 CRSP acquisition consolidates the low-cost alternative, Vanguard relationship attached

No competitor has an ETD royalty stream remotely the size of S&P DJI's SPX/E-mini complex ($324M, 18% of segment revenue; record 3.9M SPX options ADV in 2025, 59% 0DTE — Cboe [web]). Realized-fee benchmarking shows S&P DJI monetizing linked AUM at the lowest rate of the majors (~2.5bp implied vs MSCI ~3.8bp, both compressing [estimate]) — the lead is volume, not price. Share counter-evidence: S&P DJI-linked AUM grew 25% in 2025 against +33.7% for the global ETF industry ($19.85T, ETFGI [web]) — a share-of-flows loss as 2025-26 flows rotated to international/EM (MSCI's home ground) and active ETFs (which pay index providers little) reached a record $1.86T▪; buyer concentration is demonstrated (Vanguard 2012; FTSE's 2024 mandate loss); and providers' "benchmarked assets" marketing figures overstate the monetizable pool by 2-6x — the relevant sizing is the $7.2B fee pool, of which S&P DJI already has ~26%.

4.3 Financial market data & analytics (Market Intelligence)

The measured market reached $49.2B in 2025 (+6.5%), with Bloomberg (~30% share, ~$13.3B 2024 revenue [web, press estimates]) and LSEG Data & Analytics together holding about half (Burton-Taylor via trade press [web]).▪ Management's "$80B+ addressable market" is ~1.6x the measured spend — a definitional aspiration; the 2022 version of the slide cited Burton-Taylor while claiming $70B.

Player Scale KPI Line revenue Share of measured market [estimate] Growth track Margin
SPGI Market Intelligence 350,000 Cap IQ Pro users; 6T+ data points; WSO $4T+ AUM; ClearPar the settlement standard $4,916M FY2025 ~10.0%, flat-to-down since 2023 ~6% organic each of FY23/24/25 and Q1'26 20.2% GAAP / 34.0% adj
Bloomberg (private) >355,000 terminals [web] ~$13,300M 2024 [web] ~30% +6.4% 2024 [estimate] n/d
LSEG D&A ASV +5.9% at Dec 2025 £3,978M ≈ $5,090M [web] ~10-19% (perimeter-dependent) +5.0% 2025 n/d
Moody's Analytics ARR $3,607M (+8%, Mar 2026) $3,599M (+9%) ~7.3% fastest scaled peer, ~2-3pp above MI 33.1% adj
FactSet Organic ASV +6.7% (Feb 2026); 237,324 users $2,322M (FY-Aug-25) ~4.7% re-accelerating past MI's ~6% 32.2% GAAP
AlphaSense (private) ARR >$600M (Q1 2026, from $500M Oct 2025); 7,000+ clients; $7.5B valuation [web] ~$600M ARR ~1.2% and rising ~+20% in ~5 months — roughly 3x incumbent rate n/d
Versana (bank consortium) 14 top-tier banks on platform [web] pre-revenue — rapid onboarding 2023-2026 —

The per-seat gap is the cleanest expression of MI's desktop position: Bloomberg monetizes ~$37K per terminal per year; SPGI's entire MI segment divided by its 350,000 Cap IQ Pro users is ~$14K — and that numerator includes workflow and ratings-resale revenue unrelated to the desktop [estimate: division of disclosed totals].▪ The user bases are near-identical and the revenue gap is 2.7x. Counter-evidence, ranked:

  • No share gain: management's own scorecard concedes it — the 2022 target of 7-9% growth in a 5-6% market delivered ~6%, and the 2025 reset to 6-8% re-asserts share-taking with a zero-for-one record.▪
  • AlphaSense: scaling at ~3x the incumbent rate into the research workflow where Cap IQ Pro lives.
  • Versana: the first credible collective attack on the loan-workflow niche, mounted by the agent banks that feed it data.▪
  • Private-markets incumbents: BlackRock (Preqin, $3.2B) and Morningstar (PitchBook, $672M revenue, licensed users flat) are already positioned in the ~$8B private-markets data pool MI paid $1.8B (With Intelligence) to enter.

Running the other way: the AI-channel displacement wins (a 20% premium switch; 35-45% renewal uplifts) are the first competitive wins MI has cited in years, on a sample of two.

4.4 Energy and Mobility

Commodity price assessments: Platts is the clear #1 PRA — 15,000+ daily assessments, 250+ IOSCO benchmarks, the management-claimed ~70% of waterborne crude — with Argus Media the #2 and fastest gap-closer (UK-entity turnover +27.9% in FY Jun-2024 [web, weak source]; the Aramco ASCI win its flagship precedent).▪ On scale: the whole Energy segment is roughly 5-7x Argus group revenue; on the comparable price-reporting basis, the Platts price-assessment line (~$740-780M [estimate: 2022E mix rolled forward]) is roughly 2x Argus group. News Corp sizes the "energy information market" at ~$8B growing 8-10%/yr [web], on which SPGI Energy holds ~29% [estimate, perimeter caveats]. Upstream E&P data: the one pool where SPGI trails — Enverus (>$500M ARR, relationships with 98% of US producers [web]) out-scales S&P's ~$415-440M line, and SPGI chose divestiture-plus-repositioning (software to SLB, CERA Titan) over contesting workflow software.▪ US vehicle history: a two-player market in which CARFAX (38B records, 40,000 dealers) dominates Experian AutoCheck (~11B+ records [web]; that count could not be re-verified — Experian's page no longer shows it); the legacy Polk registration business provides core services to >90% of US market volume (management claim).▪ Forecasting/dealer software: S&P Mobility leads OEM forecasting (99% of global production covered) against GlobalData/LMC, a "low-cost alternative"; automotiveMastermind is a niche share-taker against Cox Automotive's ~$7B vertical stack [web, weak]. Mobility's TAM claims ($6B core B2B with a path to $18B; $26B extended CARFAX) are management-defined upsell wallet, not uncontested territory — 100% of top-40 OEMs are already customers with penetration of sellable content below 50%.

5. Value chain & profit pools

SPGI sits at the benchmark/IP layer of four chains, and the margin ladder across its own positions summarizes the profit-pool split: the benchmark layer earns 64-69% operating margins (Indices consolidated 68.7%, Ratings 63.8%), the vertical-data layer earns 41% GAAP (Energy), and the aggregation layer earns ~20% GAAP / 34% adjusted (MI) — and the aggregation layer is the only one whose suppliers are gaining pricing power.▪

The passive-investing dollar (Indices). An end investor in SPY pays a 9.45bp expense ratio; 3.0bp plus $600K/yr goes to S&P DJI — roughly a third of everything the investor pays (SPY filings, archived from EDGAR).▪ At the cheapest tier the fee is a fraction of that: VOO's ~3bp total expense ratio caps its license fee at plausibly ≤1-1.5bp [estimate]. The realized-rate mix-down is a consequence of customer economics, not lost accounts. On the derivatives leg, CME pays the JV a profit share ($193M in 2025, ≈$0.18 per S&P/Dow-complex contract [estimate]) and receives back $322M as 27% owner — the licensee is a net collector of ~$129M before its own trading economics.▪ That circularity is by design: CME's exclusive S&P 500 futures license is ownership-linked (it lapses one year after CME's JV stake drops below 5%), CME is the JV's licensing agent, and CME holds the put on its 27%. Neither side can renegotiate: the license and the ownership stake are contractually tied to each other. Cboe's SPX-options exclusivity runs to end-2032 (CBOE 10-K FY2025).

The debt-issuance dollar (Ratings). Customer ROI is overwhelming: a $1,000M 10-year IG issue pays ~$571K once at the realized 5.7bp rate — about 0.6bp/yr of par, ~1% of one year's interest cost [estimate: realized rate applied to a representative deal] — and two big-agency ratings are effectively market-access infrastructure for index-eligible debt.▪ A +3.1% list increase faces no customer-economics ceiling; the revenue risk is volume, never fee resistance. Power at this interface is flowing toward SPGI (list escalation, realized price capture disclosed in three consecutive disclosure periods); the structural counterweight is regulation (EU CRA3 already constrains pricing conduct).

The commodity-benchmark dollar (Platts). Market participants contribute price data at no disclosed fee; SPGI packages assessments and charges subscribers plus exchange royalties. The raw-material cost of the benchmark layer is approximately zero — which is why the benchmark businesses hold the chain's highest margins. The 10-K's own reputation risk names the exposure: contributors could collude to manipulate inputs.

The data-aggregation dollar (MI). Upstream payments identified in filings: AWS ($1.0B five-year commitment, ~$200M/yr — operational concentration rather than financial lock-in), third-party data suppliers (no dollars disclosed, but the 10-K states supplier consolidation "has... resulted in an increase in fees charged" and that many suppliers are also competitors), and the $172M internal Ratings royalty.▪ MI's true gross margin on purchased inputs cannot be computed from disclosure.

Constraints and float. No inventory, no backlog, no capacity constraint, no capex race anywhere in the filings; the only capacity language in two years of transcripts is Ratings analyst staffing. The chain's float sits with SPGI: customers prepay $4,088M of unearned revenue (~27% of annual revenue) while SPGI makes no capacity prepayments upstream. The one live circular flow to watch: Q1 2026 IG billed issuance rose 41% on AI-related issuance while bank-loan billed issuance declined on AI-disruption concerns — the same technology cycle is simultaneously Ratings' marginal growth driver, a credit-risk theme suppressing its leveraged-loan book, and (via AWS) SPGI's largest disclosed supplier obligation.

6. Secular & industry trends

The issuance cycle vs the trend. Disclosed billed issuance: $2,338B (2022) → $4,327B (2025, record), a +85% recovery that produced most of the 2024-25 headline growth. The through-cycle decomposition strips the cycle out: Ratings transaction revenue grew +2.3%/yr peak-to-peak (2021→2025) and −2.1%/yr trough-to-trough (2018→2022); the durable part is the non-transaction annuity (+5.5%/yr over 2017-2025, worst years −0.3% and −1.9%) plus disclosed pricing.▪ Support under the current cycle: an $8.2T rated-corporate maturity wall over 2026-2028, +26% vs the historical average 3-year wall (Investor Day 2025 deck) — the transcript's "about 9% higher over 2024" uses a different comparator (the prior-year wall). Management's own 2026 path has billed issuance decelerating in Q3 and turning negative y/y in Q4 on record compares.

Private credit, reframed. Private credit was framed as a threat that disintermediates rated public debt; SPGI now sells into it: enterprise private-markets revenue exceeded $600M in FY2025 and Ratings private-credit revenue grew 25% in Q1 2026, against a market Preqin projects from $2.28T to ~$4.5T by 2030 [web].▪ The offset runs through the bank-loan book (billed issuance down high-teens in Q1 2026), private ratings are fee-negotiated and shopping-prone, and the volume incumbent is KBRA — the net arithmetic is not disclosed. Q1 2026 also recorded the first stress language: "private credit navigate[d] increased scrutiny, wider spreads and elevated redemptions" (Q1 2026 call, CEO).

Passive flows and the compressing fee rate. The active-to-passive shift persists (record $2.37T industry ETF inflows in 2025, ETFGI [web]), but S&P DJI's share of global ETF assets is flat-to-down over nine years (28.8% end-2016 → 27.6% end-2025 [estimate]) and the realized fee rate declines ~5-6%/yr — revenue elasticity to AUM is ~0.65. The 10-12% Indices target embeds 5-7% annual market appreciation; in a flat-market year the same algebra produces roughly 4-6% growth [estimate: removing the appreciation assumption].

AI, four ways at once:

  • As distribution: MCP/plug-in channels, 300+ API customers, 35-45% renewal uplifts on two contracts — priced through enterprise renewals, not per-call fees.
  • As substitution: the feared scenario is in the company's own filings, which roughly tripled the AI risk block in FY2025 (third-party model dependency, data dilution, AI-talent competition, off-the-shelf models lowering entry barriers) — but in the disclosed numbers it remains a scenario, not a datapoint: ACV growth stable at ~6%, renewal rates rising.
  • As an end-market force: hyperscaler capex debt lifted IG billed issuance 41% in Q1 2026 while AI-disruption fear cut tech-adjacent leveraged loans — AI is currently a net Ratings demand tailwind with a mix cost.▪
  • As a cost lever: the one quantified program is the Enterprise Data Office (~$500M cost base, ~20% reduction over two years ≈ $100M, plus $10M+ realized research savings — Q4 2025 call); the CFO said on the Q1 2026 call that broad frontier-model upside "will have an impact in '27, '28."

Energy expansion; regulation. Management reframed "energy transition" to "energy expansion" (datacenter power demand; electricity demand ~3x by 2050 per its own forecast). The 2026 Energy guide was nonetheless cut to 4.5-6% on the Iran conflict and sanctions — the second consecutive year of downward in-year Energy revisions — while the volatility-geared lines produced the quarter's growth (GTS ~+30%, royalties +27%, record CERAWeek).▪ Benchmark regulation (EU/UK BMR), the EU ESG-ratings regime (from mid-2026), DORA and the AI Act add compliance cost and simultaneously entrench incumbents.

Early-warning dashboard (what would show first if the secular story broke):

  • MI: ACV below 6% for two consecutive quarters with renewal rates declining.
  • Indices: realized-price decline steepening beyond −5-7%/yr, or linked AUM visibly lagging industry ETF assets.
  • Ratings: non-transaction growth below ~5% (Q1 2026: +11%).
  • Energy: subscription growth stuck below ~7% after the conflict laps.
  • AI adoption: the metrics management chose (API customers, call volumes, ACV premia) disappearing from the script.

7. Critical points

  1. The 2026 de-rating is a multiple event, not an estimate event. From the 2025-08-14 all-time high ($564) to 2026-02-11 ($391) the stock declined 30.7% while FY2026 consensus EPS rose 3.8% over the twelve months to 2026-07-01; the forward P/E compressed from ~30x (July 2025) to 18.4x (June 2026), below the 23.9x decade median, inside a sector move of ~−30% price / ~−40% multiples (Goldman Sachs sector report, 2026-04-26; BBG series). The bear case the market priced — AI substitution of the data businesses — appears in SPGI's disclosed numbers so far only as a scenario: MI ACV growth ~6% and stable, renewal rates rising, subscription revenue growing, while the same AI cycle added issuance demand (§6). The company's own FY2025 10-K, which roughly tripled the AI risk block, is the most candid statement of the risk in the record.

  2. Half the recent growth was the issuance cycle, and the compare is now the record. Billed issuance rose 85% from the 2022 trough to $4,327B (2025); of the $2,839M of 2023→2025 revenue growth, 53% came from the ~31% of revenue that is market-geared. Through-cycle transaction growth is +2.3%/yr peak-to-peak. Management itself guides billed issuance to decelerate through 2026 and turn negative y/y in Q4; consensus CY2027-28 EPS growth of +13%/+12% requires re-acceleration after that (§14).

  3. The moat is real but it lives in Ratings, Indices, Platts and CARFAX — not evenly across the company. Benchmarks are the majority of revenue and over 70% of operating income (Investor Day 2025); MI, 32% of revenue and the division under AI attack, is the least differentiated (12% undifferentiated by management's own attribution, plus 23% resold Ratings content) and holds no margin premium over its closest comps. The enterprise ">95% proprietary" framing averages regulated oligopolies together with a contested data business.

  4. Ratings converts revenue swings into profit swings almost one-for-one, in both directions. Cycle-through incremental margin of 80% (2016→2025), up-year incrementals of 68-105% from 2019 (56% in 2017), and a 91% decremental in 2022 that removed 9.4pp of margin in one year. A 2022-scale transaction decline from the 2025 base would remove roughly $1.1B of revenue and ~$1.0B of operating profit [estimate: −45% × transaction revenue at ~90% decremental], taking the segment margin to the mid-50s.

  5. Indices compounds volume against a persistently falling fee rate, and only 73% of it is SPGI's. Realized asset-linked pricing compounded down ~6%/yr over nine years, with up-years in 2019 and 2022, while linked AUM tripled; the base effect has dominated 3:1, but a flat-market year leaves the 65%-of-revenue asset-linked line growing roughly zero. Every dollar of Indices profit is worth 73 cents to SPGI holders, and the $4.9B redeemable NCI is the largest contingent cash call on the balance sheet.

  6. The IHS Markit merger is being partly unwound four years after close. Disclosed exits (Mobility + Engineering Solutions) equal ~46% of IHSM's FY2021 revenue, likely >55% including the undisclosed exits [estimate: bounds 46-60%]; synergy execution over-delivered ($619M cost vs the $480M original target; $355M revenue run-rate a year early), and portfolio logic did not. Returns on the acquired capital run ~8.5% (Energy) and 2.3-4.1% (Mobility) against an 8-9% hurdle (§3.4, §11).

  7. Capital returns exceeded free cash flow by ~$7.5B over the decade, and the share count is still higher than in 2016. $29.8B of buybacks retired 92.5M shares over 2016-2025, but the merger issued 113.8M shares at an effective ~$381; shares outstanding rose from 265.3M (Jan 2016) to 296.0M (Apr 2026), +11.6%. Post-merger repurchases (60.4M shares, ~53% of the issuance, at a ~$414 dollar-weighted average) sit approximately break-even against the current price; the 2024-Q1 2026 tranche ($9.3B at ~$497) is ~18% underwater (§10).

  8. The whole operating leadership is new since November 2024. CEO (Cheung, internal), CFO (Aboaf, external, after a year with no permanent CFO), and the heads of MI, Ratings, Indices, Energy and Mobility all changed within ~12 months, two division presidents left involuntarily, and the say-on-pay vote dipped to 68.8% (2025) before recovering to 94.2% (2026). Execution through the spin, the AI transition, and the issuance compare rests on a team mostly under two years in seat (§10).

  9. The spin remakes the company on the run date. Mobility Global (MBGL) separated 2026-07-01; SPGI loses its most counter-cyclical consumer asset (CARFAX) and ~$0.7B of segment profit, expects ~$2B of SpinCo-raised cash, lifts 2026 buybacks to at least 100% of adjusted FCF (~$4.5B), and will re-guide ex-Mobility in H2 2026 — the recast that re-anchors every level in this dossier (§20).

  10. Watch the frontier of Ratings, not the core. US CLO share 60.5%→47.0% and CMBS 38.1%→13.0% in two years, KBRA leading private-credit volume, bank-loan billed issuance down high-teens in Q1 2026 — the marginal deal is contested even while the aggregate moat (93.4% big-three count share, HHI ~3,600) is intact. Cross-referenced as variant perception V2.

8. Variant perceptions

The bear side throughout this section is reconstructed from price action, the company's own risk-factor changes, and call Q&A — no SPGI-specific short thesis exists in the available sources, so arguments a short might add are absent (§20).

V1. "SPGI beats its guidance through execution" — the beats are the transaction cycle. Initial-guide EPS beats of +1.2% / +13.2% / +4.1% (2023/24/25) came with billed-issuance beats vs initial guides of +4pts / +45pts / +9-10pts, while the subscription-led division (MI) landed at or below the low end of initial revenue guides in 2023-2024 and missed its margin guidance both years. The symmetric tail is 2022: guidance suspended in June, EPS −16.5% vs the initial guide. Management's word is worth roughly ±1pt on subscription lines and ±10-45pts on issuance-linked lines.

V2. "The ratings duopoly is untouchable" — true at the core, false at the marginal deal. The aggregate structure is intact (93.4% big-three count share; erosion <0.5pp/yr), but in the growth categories the five-player fight is real and S&P is losing ground: CLO −13.5pp and CMBS −25.1pp of share in two years, KBRA #1 in US RMBS and in private-credit volume, DBRS and KBRA +~1pp of the revenue pool (~$120M/yr) in three years, EU share −0.8pp in one ESMA cycle. Private credit is a growth vector that is simultaneously share-contested, lower-fee, and partly cannibalistic of the bank-loan franchise (billed issuance in loans −high-teens Q1 2026).

V3. "Ratings margin expansion is a durable GenAI ratchet" — the margin tracks the cycle. The 2025 margin (63.8% GAAP) is below the 2021 peak (64.2%); 2022 demonstrated a 91% decremental; the CFO conceded cycle dependence on stage. The quantified AI cost program to date is ~$100M (EDO), with frontier-model upside guided to 2027-28. Ten years of realized price capture in nine of ten 10-Ks is the durable component; the level of margin is where revenue sits against a near-fixed cost base.

V4. "Indices is a 10-12% structural grower" — roughly half the target is market beta. Management's own guide embeds 5-7% annual appreciation; realized pricing compresses ~6%/yr (the model's own sensitivity prices −6%/yr vs −4%/yr at −$244M of terminal-year operating income); in a flat-market year the algebra gives ~4-6% growth. The durable non-market component — flows, ETD royalties, data subscriptions — is mid-single-digit. Separately, the quoted 69% margin is not SPGI's: net of the CME/CGIS 27%, the margin is 51.3% and ~$350M/yr of segment contribution difference rides on which figure is used.

V5. "AI distribution raises the value of MI's contracts" — monetization is visible only in anecdotes so far. For: 300+ API customers, call volume 5x q/q, two 35-45% renewal uplifts, a 20%-premium displacement win, AI-cohort ACV growth +30% relative with retention +200bp (MI) / +500bp (Energy). Against: aggregate ACV growth ticked down to ~6% from 6.5-7% while usage grew 5x — under enterprise pricing, usage is given away first; the anecdotes are n≤2, management-selected, without cohort controls or third-party corroboration. The market's 31% multiple de-rating on rising estimates is the bear case in price; no covering analyst's published numbers reflect it (all still model 6-8% MI medium-term growth).

V6. "The IHS Markit merger is a proven success because synergies over-delivered" — measured on portfolio outcomes it is a partial thesis reversal. Cost synergies beat ($619M vs $480M original) and revenue synergies arrived early ($355M run-rate Q3 2025), but ~46% of the acquired revenue base (disclosed: Mobility $1,747M + Engineering Solutions ~$388M annualized vs IHSM FY2021 revenue $4,659M [web]) — and likely >55% with the undisclosed exits [estimate] — is out or leaving within five years of close. Engineering Solutions was sold at a $120M pre-tax loss, the only segment-scale realized loss in the record (QuantHouse, −$31M in 2016, is the other, minor one). The gain asymmetry locates the value creation in legacy S&P IP (J.D. Power +$728M, CUSIP +$1,342M, LCD +$607M) rather than in what was bought in 2022 (IHSM-heritage exits netted ≈+$401M pre-tax on ~$4.5B+ of proceeds).

V7. "Non-transaction revenue makes Ratings recession-proof" — nearly, not absolutely. The annuity compounded +5.5%/yr over 2017-2025 with shallow declines of 0.3% (2019) and 1.9% (2022, FX; +1% constant currency) while transaction revenue declined 45% — a durability contrast, short of "no down year." The deeper history cuts harder: 2008 total Ratings revenue declined 26%, with structured finance going from 44% of segment revenue (2007) to 14% (2013) and never recovering its mix (Investor Day 2014 deck) — the recurring share rises in a bust partly because the cyclical half disappears.

V8. "The de-rating singled out SPGI" — the de-rating is sector-wide; the estimate cuts are not universal. Trailing-1Y price returns at 2026-07-01: TRI −57%, MORN −48%, FDS −45%, PYPL −42%, VRSK −39%, EFX −38% vs SPGI −16.5%, MCO −5.8%, S&P 500 +20.2%. One-year FY2026 EPS revisions inside the group: SPGI +3.8% and MCO +8.4% positive, MORN also positive (+13.7%), while FDS (−3.7%), VRSK (−3.1%), TRI (−1.0%), EFX (−7.6%) and PYPL (−6.2%) took cuts (public comp table, pull 2026-07-01). The market is repricing the business model class; the estimate evidence separates the benchmark owners from the workflow vendors.

V9. "Realized ratings pricing is ~69-71% of list" — the ratio rests on an unverifiable list price. The realized side is solid (5.71bp FY2025, 5.79bp Q1 2026, computed from disclosures); the 8.35bp/$155K list-price anchor comes from a Morningstar summary of S&P's fee disclosure whose page, like S&P's own, blocks automated fetch [web, unverified]. The wedge mechanisms (frequent-issuer terms, jumbo caps, mix) are disclosed; the precise ratio is a reader spot-check item.

9. History & inflection points

Date Event Why it mattered
2011-09-12 Board announces breakup of McGraw-Hill (markets + education), weeks after Jana Partners (3.3%) and Ontario Teachers' (2.3%) push publicly [web] The era-defining portfolio decision, externally prompted; every later identity change flows from it
2013-02-04 DOJ FIRREA suit over 2007 CDO ratings, seeking >$5,000M; 13 states + DC follow The worst legal attack a rating agency has faced
2013-03 McGraw-Hill Education sold to Apollo for $2,400M proceeds Conglomerate exit funded buybacks and debt paydown
2013-11-01 Douglas Peterson (ex-President of S&P Ratings) becomes CEO The executive brought in to clean up Ratings takes the company
2014-Q4/2015-02 $1,552M settlement charge (DOJ, 19 states + DC, CalPERS); FY2014 settlements total $1.6B; Ratings posts a −$583M segment loss Settlement ≈30% of the demand, no admission, issuer-pays model untouched
2015-09 SNL Financial acquired, $2,225M (~9.0x sales [estimate]) Backbone of today's Capital IQ Pro
2016-04 Rebrand to S&P Global; J.D. Power sold ($1,100M, +$728M gain); SPSE/CMA sold to ICE Pure-play benchmarks/data identity completed
2018-04 Kensho acquired, ~$550M — the largest AI acquisition to date then [web] Now the base of the AI product line; no revenue ever disclosed
2020-11-30 IHS Markit all-stock merger announced: $44B EV, 0.2838 ratio, $480M cost + $350M revenue synergies promised The decade's only fully offensive mega-move
2022-02-28 Merger closes at $43.5B consideration (113.8M shares at $380.89); antitrust remedies strip OPIS, Base Chemicals, CUSIP (+$1,342M gain), LCD Total assets 15,026→61,784 ($M); share count +31.7%
2022-06 → 08 Full-year guidance suspended mid-quarter as issuance collapses; reinstated assuming billed issuance −30% to −45% Ratings revenue −26%, transaction −45% in FY2022; EPS defended with $12.0B of buybacks and incentive-comp flex
2022-12-01 Investor Day: 7-9% growth, 45-47% margins for 2025/26; sustainability $800M by 2026 (~34% CAGR) Enterprise targets later hit; MI and sustainability targets missed
2023-05-02 Engineering Solutions sold ($975M; −$120M pre-tax loss) First whole-segment exit from the merger perimeter, 14 months after close
2024-07-30 CEO succession announced: Martina Cheung effective 2024-11-01 Near-total leadership turnover follows within 12 months
2025-04-29 Mobility spin announced (with the Q1 2025 call) Reversal of "core" framing; the shrink-to-the-core pivot
2025-10-10 OSTTRA JV sold to KKR ($1.5B cash to SPGI, +$270M gain) IHSM-heritage exit cadence accelerates
2025-11-13 Investor Day (Cheung): 7-9% organic CC, 50-75bp margin/yr, double-digit EPS; division margin guidance discontinued; With Intelligence closes ($1,800M) New framework set at the top of the issuance cycle
2026-02-10 Q4 2025 print: first EPS miss in 10 quarters (−0.6%); FY2026 guide midpoint 2.3% below consensus; stock −9.7% next session — the drawdown low The AI de-rating meets the deceleration guide
2026-04-28 Q1 2026: revenue +10%, billed issuance +14% (hyperscaler-front-loaded), Energy guide cut; buyback raised to ≥100% of adjusted FCF The counter-cyclical buyback escalation — the decade's first
2026-07-01 Mobility Global (MBGL) spin completed; regular-way trading begins [web] SPGI becomes a four-segment, ~$13.6B-revenue company

Five key inflections:

  • The 2011 activist-forced breakup. The board conceded to outside pressure within ~six weeks and then executed cleanly: continuing-ops revenue compounded 6.1%/yr 2013-2019 and adjusted margin rose 680bp by 2014. The pattern set here — externally prompted portfolio change, well executed — repeats through the decade.
  • The DOJ reckoning (2013-2015). Ratings went from a −$583M segment loss (2014, settlements) to a 44.4% margin (2015) and then seven consecutive margin-expansion years to 64.2% (2021). The ratings business survived the worst attack available with no observable loss of pricing power. It is the single strongest moat datapoint in the record.
  • The IHS Markit merger (2020-2022). $43.5B in stock, a 47% share-count increase, GAAP margins permanently lower, EPS −18% in year one — against strong synergy execution and a perimeter that never stabilized: six exits and a spin within five years of close.
