  • The 2022 issuance collapse. Transaction revenue −45%; guidance suspended then reinstated; EPS defended with the balance sheet rather than restructuring (severance $289M was the extent of it). It demonstrated both the model's cyclical amplitude and management's stress response: balance sheet, not operations.
  • The Cheung succession and shrink-to-the-core (2024-2026). Within 20 months of the CEO announcement: Mobility spun, OSTTRA and EDM/thinkFolio sold, ~25% of Upstream contracted to SLB, With Intelligence bought, Commodity Insights renamed Energy, a new CFO, new division heads everywhere, and reset medium-term targets — the conglomerate the prior management built is being unwound while the market prices the remaining core at a lower multiple than the conglomerate fetched.

Pattern extraction:

  • Portfolio change is externally prompted. Every trajectory-defining portfolio change was externally prompted (activists 2011, DOJ 2015, antitrust remedies 2022); the one fully self-initiated mega-move is the one being partly unwound.
  • Stress response is the balance sheet, not operations. Countercyclical buybacks have been well timed against the company's own stock.
  • Windfalls are monetized and then talked down. Deceleration was pre-announced after both the 2021 and 2025 records.
  • Superseded strategies disappear rather than get reconciled. The ESG/sustainability program went from 17-42 mentions per call (2020-21) to zero (2025-26) with the $800M-by-2026 target (~48% attained) recorded only in the proxy's compensation scorecard; the Vitality Index was retired at target; the 2018 China rating-agency greenfield vanished from all subsequent materials without explanation; Mobility was "core" until it was not.
  • Implication: current headline metrics (AI ACV premia, private-markets revenue) should be tracked with the expectation that they would be redefined if they disappoint.

10. Management, incentives & capital allocation

Leadership. Martina Cheung, CEO since 2024-11-01 — internal, 14 years at the company (Chief Strategy Officer, President MI 2019-21, President Ratings 2022-24). Eric Aboaf, CFO since 2025-02-19 — external (State Street CFO ~8 years), hired after the company ran most of 2024 with an interim CFO; package included a $2.4M signing bonus and $5.9M of make-whole awards (70% PSUs). Division leadership turned over almost completely within 12 months of the CEO change: new heads at MI (Saha), Ratings (Le Pallec), Indices (Clay, ex-Cboe, after Draper's involuntary termination), Energy (Ernsberger), Mobility (Eager, spun out with MBGL), plus two new enterprise offices (Chief Client Office; Enterprise Data Office). Ten of twelve executive officers are new in role since November 2024 (10-K FY2025, executive-officer list). The bench filled most seats internally; the concentration of newness is itself the risk.

Compensation design. Three plan metrics, all company-defined non-GAAP "ICP Adjusted" measures: revenue, EBITA margin, diluted EPS. Short-term incentive: 70% financials / 30% a qualitative "business-building scorecard"; long-term: 70% PSUs on a single metric — 3-year cumulative ICP Adjusted EPS, redesigned in 2024 from a CAGR fixed at grant to additive annual targets, which institutionalizes annual re-basing. There is no return-on-capital metric anywhere in the program — a company that spent $43.5B of stock on a merger has no ROIC accountability in pay, and EBITA excludes acquisition amortization — and no relative-TSR component (explicitly disclaimed in the proxy). The design rewards per-share EPS (and therefore buybacks), organic revenue (acquisitions are excluded from the bonus revenue metric in year one), and margin.

Does the plan bite? Yes, in both directions. 2022 funded the enterprise financial component at 57% (Ratings division at 0%) and the merger-era PSU cycle paid 40.07%; 2024 funded at 166.8% and the trough-based 2023-25 cycle paid 182.2%. Five consecutive PSU cycles paid 198 / 87 / 64 / 40 / 182 — base-year selection and cycle timing drive more of the payout variance than execution. The cushions: the 30% qualitative scorecard has funded at or above 100% in every observed year including 2022 (its only sub-100 category was Growth & Innovation at 75% in 2025, on "Underperformance in Energy Transition and Sustainability revenue"), and ICP adjustments (FX, M&A relief, Russia, divestitures) give the committee wide discretion. The 2022 "Founders Grants" ($10M to the CEO, similar to others) were performance-labeled but vested on a $480M synergy hurdle set 20% below the company's own already-raised $600M public target and 22% below what had been delivered a year before vesting. The Kansler separation package (2x cash severance plus full equity acceleration beyond the standard plan) took say-on-pay to 68.8% in 2025; the 2026 vote recovered to 94.2% (8-K Item 5.07, filed 2026-05-21), so the episode was package-specific.

Ownership. All 22 directors and executive officers together own 100,468 shares — 0.03% of the company; Cheung's direct stake is ~$12M [estimate] against an ~$18M single-year pay target. Wealth exposure runs through unvested PSUs, not owned shares. One datapoint runs the other way: Cheung bought 2,322 shares (~$1.0M) in the open market on 2026-04-29, the day after the Q1 call, with the stock down roughly a fifth from its high (stocktitan.net, accessed 2026-07-01 [web]). It is the only NEO open-market purchase in nine years of proxy records. The full Form 4 record was not pulled; other executives' 2025-26 activity is unverified (§20).

Candor. Across 24 earnings transcripts (Q2 2020-Q1 2026) there is effectively no management self-criticism: misses are attributed to markets, and course corrections are executed decisively but never narrated as corrections (the MI president was terminated within five weeks of a call that attributed MI's slowdown entirely to end markets; the Mobility "what changed?" question got spin mechanics, not an answer). Vocabulary rotates with the market: ESG from 17-42 mentions per call (2020-21) to zero (2025-26); AI from ~0-2 to 46-67. The redeeming feature is the disclosure apparatus — payout tables, guidance bridges, KPI series — which records the misses the spoken account does not concede.

Capital allocation, ten years (uses of cash, 2016-2025, from consolidated cash-flow statements):

$M OCF Capex Acquisitions (net) Disposal proceeds Buybacks Dividends Distributions to NCI
2016 1,560 115 177 1,498 1,123 380 116
2017 2,016 123 83 2 1,001 421 111
2018 2,064 113 401 6 1,660 503 154
2019 2,776 115 91 85 1,240 560 143
2020 3,567 76 201 18 1,164 645 194
2021 3,598 35 99 16 0 743 227
2022 2,603 89 (210) 3,509 12,004 1,024 270
2023 3,710 143 296 1,014 3,301 1,147 280
2024 5,689 124 305 168 3,301 1,134 287
2025 5,651 195 2,023 1,549 5,001 1,170 321
Total 33,234 1,128 3,466 7,865 29,795 7,727 2,103

Buybacks plus dividends of $37,522M equal 125% of company-defined FCF ($30,003M) — returns exceeded internally generated free cash by ~$7.5B, funded by disposal proceeds ($7.9B; portfolio M&A was a net cash source of $4.4B because the one transformational purchase used stock) plus the 2021 cash hoard and, at the margin from December 2025, commercial paper. Capex ran 0.4-1.3% of revenue every year — organic investment is expensed or acquired.

Buyback record. Execution has been programmatic: in eight of nine active years the average price paid sat within ±6% of the year's average close (fixed-dollar ASRs tracking VWAP), and pace followed proceeds (J.D. Power 2016, merger remedies 2022, OSTTRA/EDM 2025-26), not price. The two best buying windows of the decade were sat out: nothing was bought from March 2020 (stated reason: "near-term market uncertainty, including the pending US election") through the 2021 merger pause, and the resumption came in March 2022 at $362 — ~24% above the post-crash 2020 average. The first explicitly price-conditioned action in the record is February 2026: "we believe the current share price reflects an attractive opportunity to increase our repurchases... to at least 100% [of adjusted FCF] or to roughly $4.5 billion" (Q1 2026 call, CFO); the February ASR bought at $426.70, ~8% below the quarter's average, and Q1 pace ran +54% y/y. Marked to the current ~$408: the post-merger program (~$414 dollar-weighted) is approximately break-even; the 2022 tranche (~$358) is +14%; the 2024-Q1 2026 tranche (~$497) is ~18% underwater.

Per-share outcomes. Pre-merger, per-share metrics compounded at nearly the enterprise rate (shares −2%/yr): FCF/share +11.8%/yr over 2017-2025. Post-merger, enterprise growth of 10-17%/yr converted to only 4-7%/yr per share, because the merger's share issuance is still being repurchased at prices above the ~$381 effective issue price. Dividends: 52 consecutive years of increases, but the raises expose the strain — +1.1% (2024) and +1.0% (2026, to $0.97/quarter) are the smallest of the decade; the payout is deliberately junior to buybacks (~23% of FCF).

Framework vs behavior. Stated: ~85% of adjusted FCF returned "in a typical year," leverage 2.0-2.5x, dividend payout 20-25% of adjusted net income, "not interested in transformational M&A" (Investor Day 2025). Revealed: 145% of adjusted FCF returned since the start of 2022 ($21B buybacks + $4B dividends vs $17B FCF, per the company's own slide); the 2020-21 suspension missed the then-75% target two years running, when prices were lowest; and no numeric M&A hurdle rate has ever been articulated in the 2016-2026 transcript record — nothing in the framework would have flagged that issuing $43.5B of stock at ~$381 and repurchasing $24.6B at ~$414 is value-neutral at best. The quantum of return is delivered; the price discipline of the buyback is the persistent weakness, with February 2026 the first counter-example.

11. M&A, divestitures & deal track record

S&P Global is a disciplined seller and a mixed, but not value-destroying, buyer. The selling record is unusually good: large positive gains on almost every disposition it controlled the timing of — $1.1B pre-tax on the 2016 pruning, $1.9B on the 2022 remedy sales (CUSIP alone: $1,925M price, $1,342M gain, ~11x revenue), $270M on OSTTRA, $172M on EDM/thinkFolio — and zero goodwill impairments in ten years across $36.5B of goodwill. The buying record has one defining bet, the $43.5B all-stock IHS Markit merger, which over-delivered on synergies and is being partly reversed on portfolio: disclosed exits equal ~46% of IHSM's FY2021 revenue and likely more than half including the undisclosed pieces [estimate: bounds 46-60%; IHSM standalone revenue is web-sourced — its 10-Ks were not pulled this run], with the record's only segment-scale realized loss (Engineering Solutions, −$120M pre-tax vs merger-date fair value) on an acquired unit.▪ Two cautions on the clean impairment record: the test runs at reporting-unit level where acquired businesses are commingled with healthy legacy franchises, and the Mobility spin removed $8,858M of goodwill by distribution — the market, not the auditors, now prices whether it was worth its carrying value.

Deal ledger (material transactions; n.d. = not disclosed; full ledger with provenance in the M&A workpaper):

Date Counterparty Dir. Price Multiple Target at deal Goodwill / gain (loss) Outcome
2013-03 McGraw-Hill Education → Apollo Div $2,400M proceeds n.d. n.d. — Education exit; proceeds to debt + buybacks
2015-09 SNL Financial Acq $2,225M cash ~9.0x sales [estimate]; ~6.8x net of tax asset rev ~$246M [estimate] goodwill $1,574M Backbone of Cap IQ Pro; seller had paid $450M EV in 2011
2016-09 J.D. Power → XIO Div $1,100M n.d. n.d. gain +$728M ~2.8x the reported 2005 cost [web]
2016-10 SPSE + CMA → ICE Div $425M n.d. n.d. gain +$364M Legacy pruning
2016/17 QuantHouse Div n.d. — — loss −$31M The record's other realized loss
2018-04 Kensho Acq ~$550M n.d. (revenue never disclosed) ~120 employees [web] goodwill $498M (Corporate) Unmeasurable as a deal; the AI capability base
2022-02 IHS Markit Acq $43,536M stock (EV ~$44B ann. / ~$48.4B at close [estimate]) ~10.3x sales; ~24x EBITDA [estimate] rev $4,659M FY2021; adj EBITDA margin ~43% [web] goodwill $31,456M; intangibles $18,620M (customer relationships $13,596M, 25-yr life) Synergies beat; perimeter since partly dismantled
2022 OPIS + Base Chemicals → News Corp; CUSIP → FactSet; LCD → Morningstar Div (remedies) $1,150M / $295M / $1,925M / $650M CUSIP ~11x revenue CUSIP rev ~$175M gains: CUSIP +$1,342M; LCD +$607M; OPIS/BaseChem $0 by construction $2.6B of the $4.0B remedy proceeds came from selling S&P-legacy assets
2023-05 Engineering Solutions → KKR Div $975M (~$750M after tax) ~2.5x sales [estimate] rev ~$388M annualized loss −$120M pre-tax Whole acquired segment out 14 months after close
2024 Visible Alpha in; Fincentric, PrimeOne out Acq/Div $305M net in; n.d. out n.d. n.d. gains +$21M / +$38M Portfolio churn
2025-10 OSTTRA (50/50 JV w/ CME) → KKR Div $3.1B EV; $1.5B cash to SPGI n.d. below-the-line equity income gain +$270M IHSM-heritage JV exit
2025-11 With Intelligence Acq $1,800M cash ($1B new debt) ~13.8x expected 2025 revenue of $130M [web] high-teens ACV growth within $1,487M MI goodwill adds The richest disclosed multiple in the record; the private-markets bet
2026-01 EDM + thinkFolio → STG Div n.d. — — gain +$172M pre-tax "Not material" framing vs a large realized gain
2026 (agr.) Upstream software → SLB Div n.d., "not material" — ~25% of Upstream revenue — Closing 2H26/early 2027; SLB becomes a distribution partner
2026-07-01 Mobility spin (MBGL) Div (spin) tax-free distribution; ~$2B SpinCo debt with cash to SPGI planned — FY2025 rev $1,747M, ~40% adj EBITDA removes $8,858M goodwill Completed on the run date

Synergy track record. Cost: $480M promised (Nov 2020), raised to $600M (Dec 2022), delivered $619M run-rate by end-2023 — against roughly $1.2-1.5B of one-time costs to achieve, a sub-3-year payback. Revenue: $350M by year five, run-rate $355M by Q3 2025, "well in advance of the time line," then retired from reporting; the 2024 phasing was trimmed when Engineering Solutions left, and no disclosure states whether the target was rebased for the other divested units. Deck-only deal detail: the merger priced at 28.2x LTM EBITDA pre-synergies / 19.6x post-synergies by management's own announcement slide, and management's stated capital philosophy is that a point of revenue growth creates ~5x the enterprise value of a point of margin (Investor Day 2018 deck, citing HOLT). That frame is what produced a growth-priced mega-merger.

12. Capitalization & balance sheet

All debt is senior unsecured fixed-rate notes issued by S&P Global Inc., guaranteed by Standard & Poor's Financial Services LLC, plus commercial paper. No secured debt, no term loans, no material finance leases (10-K FY2025, Note 5; 10-Q 2026-Q1, Note 4).

Instrument Coupon Maturity Book 3/31/26 $M
Senior notes 2.95% 2027 499
Senior notes 2.45% 2027 1,247
Senior notes 4.75% 2028 781
Senior notes 4.25% 2029 988
Senior notes 2.5% 2029 498
Sustainability-linked notes 2.95% (stepped +25bp 3/1/2026) 2029 1,241
Senior notes 1.25% 2030 596
Senior notes 4.25% 2031 596
Senior notes 2.90% 2032 1,480
Senior notes 5.25% 2033 744
Senior notes 4.80% 2035 396
Senior notes 6.55% 2037 291
Senior notes 4.5% 2048 273
Senior notes 3.25% 2049 591
Senior notes 3.70% 2052 976
Senior notes 2.3% 2060 683
Senior notes 3.9% 2062 487
Commercial paper floating (~4.35% [estimate]) <1 yr 951
Total debt 13,318

Weighted-average cost of debt: principal-weighted coupon 3.45% on $12,268M of notes at 3/31/2026; ~3.52% including CP at an estimated 4.35%. Cash interest paid was $390M in FY2025; GAAP interest expense runs ~5-10bp below cash coupon because a ~$292M merger fair-value premium amortizes as a credit. 92.8% of debt is fixed-rate.

Maturity wall and refinancing cost. 2027: $1.7B at a blended 2.59%; 2028: $784M at 4.75%; 2029: $2.7B at ~3.3%; 2030: $596M at 1.25%; thereafter $6.5B. SPGI's own December 2025 prints (4.25% 6-year, 4.80% 10-year) mark the refinancing curve: rolling everything due through 2030 adds roughly $65-75M of annual pre-tax interest by 2030 [estimate] — about 1% of FY2025 operating profit. Debt fair value ($11.3B) sits $1.8B below book because the embedded coupons (the 2020-issued 1.25% 2030s and 2.3% 2060s above all) are far below market. Refinancing is a cost drag, not a solvency question: interest coverage is 22.6x, the single covenant (indebtedness/cash flow ≤4:1) has never been exceeded, and liquidity is ~$3.8B (cash $1,810M plus a $2.0B undrawn revolver to December 2029). The pension is frozen and overfunded (+$81M); leases are small ($618M liability) and shrinking.

The redeemable NCI is the biggest quasi-liability. CME/CGIS can put at least 20% of their 27% S&P DJI stake to SPGI at any time at fair value, and the whole interest on a change of control. Balance: $4,917M at end-2025, up $665M in the year (the redemption value compounds ~15%/yr with the JV; management's Level 3 valuation uses a 10.8% WACC and 2.2% terminal growth, and the 10-K warns the ultimate amount "could be significantly different"). A full put would be a ~$5B+ cash call — ~37% of gross debt — fundable at ~$225-240M/yr of interest against $322M of recovered NCI income: accretive but leverage-additive by ~0.6x. Probability is low (CME has held since 2012 and collects growing distributions, $301M in 2025); consequence is the largest on the balance sheet.

Leverage basis gap. Management quotes 2.3x gross leverage (Q1 2026) against a 2.0-2.5x target; plain GAAP arithmetic gives ~1.7x gross / 1.5x net (total debt ÷ operating income + D&A). The internal EBITDA definition is unpublished; one reconstruction that reproduces 2.3x is including the $4.9B redeemable NCI in the debt numerator. The gap matters directionally: on GAAP arithmetic the balance sheet has ~$6B of unused capacity to the top of the band; on the management basis it is near the top already.

SBC and share count. SBC is 1.5% of revenue ($236M FY2025), all restricted/performance stock, ~0.15% of shares issued per year against ~3.0% repurchased — the buyback is a capital-shrink program, not a comp offset. Share-count trajectory: 265.3M (Jan 2016) → 241.1M (Feb 2022) → ~340M (Apr 2022, merger) → 296.0M (Apr 2026). New 30M-share authorization (Nov 2025); the 2022 program completed Q1 2026.

Structural risks, ranked:

  1. The CME/CGIS put ($4.9B, growing).
  2. Refinancing cost drift (~$65-75M/yr by 2030).
  3. Capital-return commitments ahead of the spin — 2026 buybacks ≥100% of adjusted FCF (~$4.5B) plus the ~$2B Mobility dividend earmarked mostly for repurchases, with CP already funding ASRs ($951M at 3/31/2026) and management's own guide drifting leverage from 2.3x to 2.4x just as Mobility's EBITDA leaves.
  4. Floating-rate exposure minor (CP only).
  5. Structural subordination minor (<$150M of unexchanged IHSM rump notes).
  6. Covenant/liquidity negligible.
  7. FX mismatch none material (all-USD debt, hedged).

Ratings context: Moody's affirmed A3/stable at the merger (March 2022) [web]; current agency views were not verified locally.

13. Financial history & summary

Basis notes for the whole section: GAAP as recast in the latest filings; FY2022 consolidates IHS Markit from 2022-02-28 (ten months), which breaks organic comparability at that seam; segment revenue includes the Ratings→MI intersegment royalty; all actuals include Mobility, whose spin completed 2026-07-01. Forward columns are Bloomberg BEst consensus as of 2026-07-01, still Mobility-inclusive, and BEst operating income/EBITDA/net income are on an adjusted basis (they exceed the GAAP rows' definitions); consensus gross margin is on Bloomberg's cost tagging, not the operating-related-expense construction used for the actual years. FY2016 GAAP operating income includes $1,101M of disposition gains and FY2022 includes $1,898M — the ex-gains EBITDA row restates those years.

13.3 What drove the decade

The 2016→2025 story decomposes into four moves:

  • Ratings cycle: segment operating profit went $1,262M → $2,629M (2021) → $1,672M (2022) → $3,013M (2025), and every consolidated inflection tracks it — the 2023 operating-margin trough (32.2%) combined the issuance trough with full-year merger amortization ($1,042M), merger costs ($236M) and severance.
  • Merger break: revenue rose 35% in 2022 while gross margin declined 7.3pp (IHS Markit's cost base) and D&A quintupled; the GAAP-adjusted gap has since narrowed only through denominator growth, because amortization is flat at ~$1.07B.
  • Indices scale: segment profit +$346M over 2023-2025 at a stable ~68% margin.
  • Cash conversion: FCF margin peaked at 46.9% (2020, issuance boom, minimal capex), troughed at 22.5% (2022, merger costs), and printed 35.6% in 2025 — FY2025 OCF was flat (−0.7%) against +16% net income because receivables rose $574M (DSO 74→82 days) and divestiture-gain taxes hit; Q1 2026 OCF +8.8% is a partial normalization, and whether the DSO spike was timing or structural is an open model input (§17).

Ending ETF AUM ($T) 1.309 1.696 1.998 2.796 2.601 3.303 4.389 5.480
Average ETF AUM ($T) 1.399 1.503 1.681 2.419 2.526* 2.895 3.836 4.772
Indices asset-linked fees ($M) 522 613 647 800 862 859 1,046 1,206
Realized asset-linked rate (bps, proxy) 3.73 4.08 3.85 3.31 3.41 2.97 2.73 2.53
Indices ETD royalties ($M) 171 140 165 158 219 267 290 324
Platts exchange royalties ($M) n/d 60 62 66 67 81 103 120

* 2022 average is ex-merger basis (the merger added ~$135B of ending AUM). Billed issuance dollars are disclosed only from 2022; the realized asset-linked rate is an upper-bound blend (mutual-fund and OTC fees in the numerator) whose trend, not level, is the signal.

Last four quarters (y/y basis, per earnings releases and calls — a different basis from the annual rows): billed issuance +12% (Q2'25), +12% (Q3'25), +28% (Q4'25), +14% (Q1'26); MI organic ACV growth 6-6.5% → 6.5-7% → 6.5-7% → ~6%, with net renewal rates +100bp; average ETF AUM +14% → +29% → n/d → +25%; Indices ETD revenue +18% in Q1 2026 on record SPX volumes; Energy subscription growth +8% (FY2025) decelerating to +4% (Q1 2026). Call-sourced series without filing anchors, quoted as management claims: enterprise private-markets revenue >$600M FY2025 (+16% in Q4 2025); Kensho LLM-ready API customers 300+; API call volume 5x q/q; more than a third of Cap IQ Pro users on AI features. Two discontinued KPI series: Sustainability & Energy Transition revenue was disclosed quarterly through Q4 2025 ($101M, −3% y/y; ~$383M FY2025 against an $800M-by-2026 target) and then dropped from disclosure; the Vitality Index (new-product revenue, ran 10-12%) was retired at target in the same quarter.

Key inflections the KPIs reveal: the 2022 transaction collapse and 2024 record (+54% billed issuance) bracket the whole earnings cycle; the realized asset-linked rate has declined in every year since 2019 except 2022, compounding down ~6%/yr; it is the one KPI trend management does not discuss as a trend; the Platts royalty line is the fastest compounding series in the company (~16%/yr since 2021, +27% in Q1 2026); and MI's ACV series (trough ~4% in Q4 2024, recovery to 6.5-7%, tick down to ~6% in Q1 2026) is the single series that will settle the AI-substitution question.▪

14. Expectations: internal vs. external vs. base rates

The headline disagreement: consensus prices the very top of management's guidance ($19.63 CY2026 EPS vs a $19.40-19.65 guide) and then accelerates above it (+13.2% CY2027, +12.0% CY2028) — a path that requires the issuance cycle not to bite after the 2026 record-compare year, while management itself pre-announces billed-issuance growth turning negative in Q4 2026.▪

Driver Internal (management) External (consensus) Where/why they disagree Base-rate
Enterprise organic revenue growth 7-9%/yr "annually and on average" ex-Mobility (ID 2025); 6-8% for 2026; division guide midpoints sum to ~6.7-6.8% +7.7% reported CY2026, +7.3%/yr to 2028 (~6.8% organic-equivalent) Consensus sits at the guide's mid; the ID band's floor equals the achievable mid-cycle rate, its top has no organic precedent at this scale Large-base (≥$4B revenue) info-services 5-yr forward CAGR: median +7.5% M&A-inclusive; organic ≥7% at scale ≈ top decile (2-3 of 35 windows). SPGI's own full-cycle organic rate ≈ 7%, range +3% (issuance trough) to +11% (boom)
Adjusted operating margin +50-75bp/yr ex-OSTTRA; "largest opportunity" in MI; division margin guidance discontinued from 2026 50.5% → 52.2% by CY2028 (+56bp/yr) Consensus is inside the guide — but ~1.2-1.4pp of the 3-year ask arrives mechanically from spinning 40%-margin Mobility off an ~50% base [estimate: mix arithmetic]; the operational ask net of the spin is ~30-40bp/yr Margin expansion from a ≥45% GAAP base occurred in ~24% of 5-yr windows — every one a monoline (MSCI, CME); median change −4.5pp. No multi-segment precedent
Adjusted EPS Double-digit growth medium-term; $19.40-19.65 (+9-10%) 2026 $19.63 (92nd percentile of the guide); +13.2% / +12.0% / +11.1% CY2027-29; LTG 12.8% The street's out-years assume Ratings re-accelerates after 2026 and the buyback persists; 2026's buyback is part-funded by one-time proceeds (OSTTRA $1.5B; ~$2B spin dividend) that do not repeat 88% quarterly beat rate over 41 quarters (median surprise +6.6%), but all five misses cluster at cycle turns; the guide's symmetric tail is 2022's −16.5% vs initial guide
Billed issuance / Ratings Low-to-mid single digits 2026, negative Q4; Ratings 4-7% 2026, 6-9% medium-term; maturity wall $8.2T (+26% vs history) CY2027-28 EPS growth implies re-acceleration; no division-level consensus visible Q1 2026 printed +14% and management absorbed it as hyperscaler pull-forward with no raise — part of the year's budget is pre-spent Initial issuance guides missed actuals by +4pts (2023), +45pts (2024), +9-10pts (2025) — always upside in up-years; the one down-year (2022) forced a guidance suspension. Peak-to-peak transaction growth +2.3%/yr
MI 6-8% medium-term; 5.5-7% 2026 with subscription in the top half; ACV ~6%, "building through the year" ~6% modeled; medium-term 6-8% in published numbers Little numeric disagreement — the disagreement is the multiple: the market priced structural impairment no analyst's numbers show MI grew at the measured market rate (~6% vs +6.4-6.5%) for three years; the prior 7-9% share-gain target delivered market rate — zero-for-one on share-gain claims
Indices 10-12% 2026 and medium-term, embedding 5-7% appreciation (now flat-from-April for 2026) 10-12% inherited; realized-fee-rate compression unmodeled — zero analyst questions in two quarters of calls The street models AUM beta, not the −6%/yr fee compression; the target's non-market component is mid-single-digit Fee compression of ~6%/yr compounded over nine years (up-years in 2019 and 2022); revenue elasticity to AUM ~0.65
Energy 6-8% medium-term; 2026 cut to 4.5-6% (only division cut) trimmed with the guide Management's word is weakest here: two consecutive years of downward in-year revisions; reaching 6% needs the conflict transient and CERA Titan (unlaunched) to sell Pre-conflict organic ran ~7%; franchise 5-yr CAGR ≈ 5.2% [estimate: pro-forma base]
Capital return ~85% of adjusted FCF typical; 2026 raised to ≥100% (~$4.5B buybacks); ~$2B spin proceeds mostly to repurchases Consensus builds ~2.5-3%/yr share shrink None — this is the one guidance line where the record is over-delivery (145% of FCF returned since 2022) Delivered or exceeded in 8 of 10 years; the two misses (2020-21) coincided with the decade's best prices

The management-vs-street picture reduces to one asymmetry. On revenue, the street is sober: consensus sits at the reference-class median and at SPGI's own mid-cycle rate. On EPS path, both management's band and consensus's 12% CAGR require the top half of the base-rate distribution to repeat without the issuance recovery that produced it in 2023-25 — the floor (5.5-6% organic, high-single-digit EPS) is well populated with precedents; the top (9% organic, mid-teens EPS from a $15B base, organically) has essentially none. Guidance credibility is asymmetric by line: division-level, Ratings is the chronic upside surprise ("exceeded internal expectations" in five separate quarters), MI missed margin guidance in 2023 and 2024 before hitting in 2025, Energy is the only division cut two years running, and Mobility guided cleanly throughout. The 2022 Investor Day scorecard management presents as "delivered" was mix-shifted: the enterprise targets were hit because Ratings exceeded its margin target by 400-500bp on a record cycle while MI missed its growth and margin model and the $800M sustainability target ended ~52% short and unmentioned.

15. Market pricing & valuation history

This section is a descriptive record of what earnings did, what the market paid, and why the stock moved; it contains no valuation judgment. All Bloomberg data pulled live 2026-07-01. Price levels are quoted as traded: Bloomberg back-adjusted its default SPGI history for the 2026-07-01 Mobility distribution (uniform factor ≈0.9459, pricing the distribution at ~$22 per SPGI share, ~5.4% of cum value), and both bases are preserved in the workpaper CSVs.

15.1 Ten-year multiples

Perfect-foresight forward P/E uses the next year's actual adjusted EPS (computable only with hindsight); consensus forward P/E is the BEst 12-month blended-forward multiple investors actually saw at each fiscal year-end. EV excludes the CME/CGIS redeemable NCI while EBITDA consolidates 100% of Indices, so recent-year EV/EBITDA is mildly flattered; the method is stated rather than mixed.

FY GAAP EPS Adj EPS FY-avg px FY-end px Trail P/E (GAAP) Trail P/E (adj) Fwd P/E perfect-foresight Consensus fwd P/E at FY-end EV/EBITDA (FY-end)
2016 7.94 5.35 108.83 107.54 13.5x 20.1x 15.6x 17.4x 8.2x
2017 5.78 6.89 144.69 169.40 29.3x 24.6x 19.9x 21.7x 15.9x
2018 7.73 8.50 192.00 169.94 22.0x 20.0x 17.8x 17.4x 14.8x
2019 8.60 9.53 230.95 273.05 31.8x 28.7x 23.4x 24.9x 19.2x
2020 9.66 11.69 316.68 328.73 34.0x 28.1x 24.0x 26.2x 20.1x
2021 12.51 13.70 402.86 471.93 37.7x 34.4x 42.2x 30.6x 24.8x
2022 10.20 11.19 362.62 334.94 32.8x 29.9x 26.6x 25.2x 19.6x
2023 8.23 12.60 376.42 440.52 53.5x 35.0x 28.1x 28.9x 28.5x
2024 12.35 15.70 466.72 498.03 40.3x 31.7x 27.9x 27.9x 24.5x
2025 14.66 17.83 510.15 522.59 35.6x 29.3x n/a 24.9x 22.4x

No negative-EPS year occurs in the window (the last GAAP loss was FY2014, on the ratings settlements). The GAAP-adjusted divergence is structural: FY2016 GAAP exceeds adjusted because of disposition gains; from FY2022 GAAP sits far below adjusted because ~$1.1B/yr of merger amortization entered the GAAP base (the FY2023 trailing GAAP P/E of 53.5x reflects that amortization, not price). Perfect-foresight forward P/E sat below the contemporaneous consensus multiple in 8 of 10 years — delivered EPS beat what year-end consensus embedded, except into 2022. Long-run reference: the monthly blended-forward P/E has a 23.9x median since 2015, a 16.4x low (Feb 2016), a 31.2x high (Nov 2021), and printed 18.4x in June 2026. P/B is not a relevant lens for SPGI (intangible-heavy, never traded on book; current P/B 3.9x quoted for completeness only).

15.2 Annual prices & returns (fiscal = calendar year)

FY FY-end close FY avg FY high FY low Price return Total return
2016 107.54 108.83 127.56 80.77 +9.1% +10.6%
2017 169.40 144.69 172.57 108.39 +57.5% +59.3%
2018 169.94 192.00 214.78 159.00 +0.3% +1.4%
2019 273.05 230.95 274.38 164.37 +60.7% +62.3%
2020 328.73 316.68 378.41 192.05 +20.4% +21.4%
2021 471.93 402.86 478.97 305.95 +43.6% +44.7%
2022 334.94 362.62 461.10 286.62 (29.0)% (28.4)%
2023 440.52 376.42 441.84 324.79 +31.5% +32.8%
2024 498.03 466.72 529.00 408.56 +13.1% +13.9%
2025 522.59 510.15 564.15 438.31 +4.9% +5.7%
2026 YTD (to 6/30, pre-spin) 407.26 443.11 546.35 390.76 (22.1)% (21.7)%

Ten-year total-return CAGR (end-2015 → end-2025): 19.3%, cumulative +483%. Adjusted EPS compounded 14.3%/yr over the same window, so roughly five points of the decade's annual return came from re-rating (17.4x → 24.9x on the year-end forward multiple) plus dividends — and the 2026 decline has unwound the re-rating component (18.4x at June 2026, below the decade median).

15.3 Earnings vs expectations

Quarterly record: 36 beats in 41 quarters FY2016-Q1 2026 (median surprise +6.6%); the five misses cluster at cycle turns (Q3 2019, Q2/Q4 2022, Q2 2023, Q4 2025). Initial annual guides were exceeded in every completed year shown — by +10.9% (2021), +1.2% (2023), +13.2% (2024), +4.1% (2025) — with the one large miss (2022, −16.5%, guidance suspended) in the issuance down-year; the guidance record and its cycle asymmetry are analyzed in §14.

Last 12 quarters (announcements are pre-market, so the report date is the reaction session):

Qtr Report date Rev act $M Rev cons $M Rev surp Adj EPS act EPS cons EPS surp Day reaction
2023 Q2 2023-07-27 3,101 3,059 +1.4% 3.12 3.13 (0.4)% (7.6)%
2023 Q3 2023-11-02 3,084 3,028 +1.8% 3.21 3.04 +5.5% +7.0%
2023 Q4 2024-02-08 3,152 3,124 +0.9% 3.13 3.11 +0.7% (5.0)%
2024 Q1 2024-04-25 3,491 3,414 +2.2% 4.01 3.65 +9.9% +0.5%
2024 Q2 2024-07-30 3,549 3,415 +3.9% 4.04 3.65 +10.8% (0.3)%
2024 Q3 2024-10-24 3,575 3,429 +4.3% 3.89 3.64 +7.0% (3.5)%
2024 Q4 2025-02-11 3,593 3,487 +3.0% 3.77 3.47 +8.6% +4.9%
2025 Q1 2025-04-29 3,777 3,704 +2.0% 4.37 4.20 +4.0% +2.6%
2025 Q2 2025-07-31 3,755 3,679 +2.1% 4.43 4.22 +5.0% +4.1%
2025 Q3 2025-10-30 3,888 3,832 +1.5% 4.73 4.41 +7.2% +3.9%
2025 Q4 2026-02-10 3,916 3,903 +0.3% 4.30 4.33 (0.6)% (9.7)%
2026 Q1 2026-04-28 4,171 4,074 +2.4% 4.97 4.82 +3.2% (0.9)%

Reactions beyond ±5%, with what the market reacted to:

Qtr Reaction What the market reacted to
2023 Q2 (7.6)% (S&P 500 −0.6%) An in-line print after a +31% YTD run; on the call, margin contraction as 2022 cost actions lapped, the withdrawal of the 2026 $800M sustainability-revenue timeline, and named demand headwinds from US financial institutions — MI's buyer base. FY2023 consensus moved only −0.4% over the week: a multiple move, not estimates
2023 Q3 +7.0% (S&P 500 +1.9%) +5.5% EPS beat, guidance raised, prior ASR completed and a new $1.3B ASR announced; ~2 points of the day was market beta
2023 Q4 (5.0)% (S&P 500 +0.1%) Small beat, but the initial FY2024 EPS guide ($13.75-14.00) landed ~4% below the $14.45 consensus; consensus reset −2.5% that week. The guide was ultimately beaten by 13.2%
2025 Q4 (9.7)% (S&P 500 −0.3%) First EPS miss in 10 quarters (−0.6%), the smallest revenue surprise of the 12 quarters, and an initial FY2026 guide whose midpoint sat 2.3% below consensus with organic growth guided to decelerate on record compares. FY2026 consensus reset only −1.9%; the other ~8 points was multiple compression inside the sector-wide AI de-rating. The next session marked the drawdown low

The middle of the table records the opposite pattern: four consecutive +7% to +11% EPS beats in 2024 produced day reactions between −3.5% and +4.9% — through 2024 the market largely declined to pay for beats driven by the issuance recovery, with the forward multiple drifting from ~29x toward 25x while estimates rose.

15.4 The decade's major moves

Peak Trough Depth What changed
2015-03 ($109) 2016-02 ($81) (25.6)% Credit-cycle scare: HY spreads widened, issuance stalled; estimates kept rising through it — macro repricing
2016-09 ($128) 2016-12 ($108) (15.7)% Post-election rate spike; forward P/E 20.0x → 17.4x on rising EPS — pure multiple
2018-07 ($215) 2018-12 ($159) (26.0)% Q4 2018 rate shock froze issuance; December corporate issuance effectively closed
2020-02 ($311) 2020-03 ($192) (38.3)% COVID crash; recovered fastest of the decade — issuers termed out liquidity and Ratings printed a +26.9% beat quarter
2021-12 ($479) 2022-10 ($287) (40.2)% The deepest drawdown: the 2022 rate shock collapsed billed issuance (Ratings revenue −26%) while the merger closed into it; both earnings and multiple declined. The Dec-2021 peak was not regained until July 2024
2025-02 ($544) 2025-04 ($438) (19.4)% April-2025 tariff-shock risk-off; the Mobility spin was announced into the recovery
2025-08 ($564, all-time high) 2026-02-11 ($391) (30.7)%, not recovered by 6/30 The AI de-rating of information services plus the February 2026 guide (§15.3). Through the episode the forward-EPS line rose every month: a de-rating, not an estimate collapse — unlike 2022, which was both

From the 2026-02-11 trough the stock recovered +4.2% to 6/30, then rose 8.0% on the spin-completion day (2026-07-01, distribution-adjusted basis).

15.5 Forward view (dated 2026-07-01; estimates still Mobility-inclusive)

BEst mean FY2025A FY2026E FY2027E FY2028E FY2029E
Revenue ($M) 15,326 16,523 17,725 19,015 20,213
EBITDA ($M) 7,839 8,470 9,258 10,048 10,652
Operating income ($M) 7,743 8,382 9,109 9,924 10,528
Adjusted EPS ($) 17.86 19.63 22.22 24.89 27.66
GAAP EPS ($) 14.74 17.16 19.25 21.81 25.09
Capex ($M) 189 212 221 231 229

No BEst free-cash-flow series is populated for SPGI; the FCF line is blank rather than bridged. Estimate dispersion: FY2026 σ $0.08 on a $19.63 mean (±0.4%, n=25) — the near year is settled; FY2028 σ $1.26 with a $22.45-29.00 range (26% of mean, n=20) — the out-year split is about margins, buyback pace, and the Ratings cycle, since the FY2028 revenue range is only 5% wide. Implied multiples at the 2026-06-30 close of $407.26 (clean cum-Mobility basis): 20.7x / 18.3x / 16.4x / 14.7x for FY2026-29; ±1σ on FY2028 gives 15.6x-17.3x. At the post-spin $414.97 the FY2026 multiple is 21.1x on a mixed basis (post-spin price over Mobility-inclusive EPS) — not comparable until BEst recasts. Reference points: 18.4x blended-forward at June 2026 vs the 23.9x median since 2015 and the 17.4x year-end lows of 2016/2018.

15.6 Charts

Daily as-traded close 2010-June 2026, log scale, with dated annotations (settlements, merger, 2022 trough, CEO change, Aug-2025 high, Feb-2026 guide). Source: Bloomberg via xbbg, pull 2026-07-01.

Price vs BEst 12-month blended-forward EPS scaled at the 2016-25 median forward multiple (24.9x): parallel moves are estimate-driven, gaps are re-rating. The price line crossed below the earnings line in early 2026 for the first sustained stretch since 2016-17. Source: Bloomberg, pull 2026-07-01.

Monthly blended-forward P/E 2015-June 2026 with the 23.9x median marked. Source: Bloomberg, pull 2026-07-01.

Fiscal-year total-return bars 2016-2026 YTD. Source: Bloomberg, pull 2026-07-01.

16. Public comparable-company table

Descriptive market context only; no valuation judgment. Bloomberg/xBBG pull 2026-07-01, intraday prices; universe = SPGI, company-map peers, 2026 proxy compensation peers, adjacent public information-services/exchange comps, one compounder reference (MSFT), and the S&P 500 / Nasdaq 100. SPGI's own row mixes the post-spin price with Mobility-inclusive estimates (§15.5 caveat). EXPN and LSEG prices are in GBp (market caps converted at 1.3281); Fiserv trades as FISV and Marsh & McLennan as MRSH on Bloomberg's live lines; net-debt/EBITDA is blanked for bank-balance-sheet models (AXP, STT, SCHW); MORN's FY2026E EPS reflects an apparent consensus-basis discontinuity vs its 2025A print.
