NDXNasdaq 10029,809.13USDDec——9.3x—18%32%96%—1,287.611,555.121,714.8513.8%———23x19x17x——————————

17. Forward model summary

Driver-based model, FY2026-FY2032, GAAP segment basis (MI modeled adjusted and bridged to GAAP through amortization), built bottom-up — no line is a revenue CAGR. Mobility is consolidated for H1 2026 only ($944M revenue, $210M GAAP operating profit) and excluded from 2027; no future disposal gains are forecast beyond the already-reported $172M EDM/thinkFolio gain. Full assumptions, scenario paths and the executable build are in the workpapers (model_assumptions.json, model_build.py, projections.csv). No multiples are applied to these projections and no value is derived from them.

17.1 Assumptions (base case; bear/bull in parentheses)

Driver Base (bear / bull) Tag Note
Billed issuance growth 2026 +5.0% (+2.0 / +8.0) mgmt-guidance Set above the guide mid because initial issuance guides under-shot actuals three years running; Q1 printed +14% with a pre-announced negative Q4. The bull end is narrow against the realized error distribution — 2025-style repeats (+9-11%) sit above it
Billed issuance growth 2027-2032 +4.0%/yr avg (−1.0 / +6.5; bear path −15% 2027, −5% 2028) base-rate Splits the maturity-wall case against the +2.3%/yr peak-to-peak record. The bear down-leg is half the 2022 magnitude — management's own reinstated 2022 guidance assumed issuance −30% to −45%, so a full 2022 replay (~−30%) would roughly double the ~$1.0B 2027 operating-income hit quantified below
Ratings realized fee rate +0.5%/yr (−0.5 / +1.0) historical-record List escalation net of IG/jumbo mix. The three most recent data points (FY2025 −4.0%, Q4 2025, Q1 2026) ran against this drift because the base case's own growth comes from fee-capped IG issuance; the evidence is more consistent with ~0 to −1%/yr while IG dominates, so the base is charitable on this line
Ratings non-transaction growth +6.0%/yr (+3.0 / +7.5) historical-record Nine-year CAGR +5.8%, worst year −2%, Q1 2026 +11%; royalty and Crisil compound independently
Ratings segment expense growth +4.8%/yr (+2.0 / +6.0) historical-record 1.5pp above the ten-year 3.3% CAGR; bear replays the documented incentive-comp flex (expenses −6.1% in 2022)
Average ETF AUM growth +9.5%/yr (+2.0 / +12.0) base-rate Half the realized 2017-2025 CAGR (19.3%): ~7% appreciation + 2.5pp flows; 2026 is largely locked by the Q1 carry-in (+16.8% vs the FY2025 average)
Indices asset-linked fee-rate change −4.0%/yr (−5.0 / −3.0) historical-record The one assumption the evidence contradicts as a range: the disclosed series compressed −6.1%/yr over nine years and −8.0%/yr over five, with the last two annual prints at −8.1% and −7.3% — the assumed bear is milder than every realized multi-year window. History supports base ≈−6, bear ≈−8, bull ≈−4; each 2pp of extra compression costs ~$244M of terminal-2032 operating income, so base-case Indices output (and consolidated EPS by ~1-2%) is biased high by roughly that amount
ETD royalty growth +8.0%/yr (+6.0 / +12.0) mgmt-guidance Conservative vs the 19.6% 2021-2025 CAGR; the countercyclical bear logic is evidenced (2022 +39%). The floors admit no negative year although the series printed two (2019, 2021) in calm rising markets
Index data subscriptions +7.0%/yr (+5.0 / +9.0) historical-record Whole range set below the 10.3% nine-year CAGR; accelerating recent prints
Indices segment expense growth +7.0%/yr (+5.5 / +8.5) historical-record Encodes the realized zero-free-leverage relationship (nine-year incremental ≈ in-place margin)
MI subscription organic growth +6.0%/yr (+4.0 / +8.0) consensus The print, not the 6-8% band: six quarters of ~6% ACV equals market growth; bear = the published AI-erosion case (4-6%); bull = AI renewal uplifts annualizing. ±2pp is worth ∓$867M of terminal OI — the single largest lever
MI recurring variable +6.0%/yr (+2.0 / +9.0) historical-record Volume-linked loan plumbing; bear ties its 2027 leg to the issuance bear
MI adjusted expense growth +5.2%/yr (+4.0 / +6.3) historical-record Implies ~55bp/yr adjusted-margin expansion, matching the guide with MI "solidly in the middle"; 2025 ran a 59.4% incremental
Energy revenue growth 2027+ +6.0%/yr (+3.0 / +7.5); 2026 +5.0 mgmt-guidance Set at the bottom of management's 6-8% band — the division where the guidance record is weakest (cut two years running; Q1 2026 subscription +4%). Reaching 6% requires the conflict transient and CERA Titan to sell; the evidence supports ~5-5.5% or a wider bear, so Energy's base contribution has modest downside skew
Energy expense growth +4.7%/yr (+2.8 / +5.5) historical-record Matches realized prints; caps the incremental margin at the realized ~55-57%
Mobility (H1 2026 only) +8.0% FYE-equivalent mgmt-guidance Spin assumed complete 2026-06-30 (it completed 2026-07-01); each quarter of slip would have shifted ~$470M revenue
Corporate Unallocated −$320M 2026, then −$300M/yr flat historical-record Below the recent underlying range (−$305M to −$502M; 2025 underlying ≈−$416M ex the OSTTRA gain) and it must absorb spin-stranded costs — a one-directional ~$50-115M/yr operating-income bias toward optimism in all scenarios; the evidence supports −$350 to −$400M/yr
Net interest $320M (2026) → $365M (2032) historical-record Prices the 2027/2029 walls rolling to a 4.3-4.8% curve
Tax rate 21.5% (21.8% in 2026) historical-record Center of the ex-gain 20.9-22.6% band
NCI attribution 25.3% of Indices OP + $33M historical-record Ties to FY2025 ($322M + other = $349M total NCI within 2%)
Diluted share change −2.5%/yr 2027+ (−3.8% 2026) mgmt-guidance 2026 = ~$4.5B buyback + ~$2B spin proceeds; steady state = ~85% of FCF less dividends at current prices
Capex 1.3% of revenue (1.4% 2026) historical-record Top of the ten-year 0.4-1.4% range; AI compute is contractually opex (AWS)
Working capital & other −$400M/yr OCF drag historical-record Back-tests to FY2024 within −9% and FY2025 within +3%; FCF precision ±5%. If the 2025 DSO spike is structural, base FCF is ~2-3% high
Amortization of intangibles $957M 2026, $800M 2027, −3%/yr historical-record Schedule arithmetic (Mobility out, With Intelligence in); GAAP-only line
SBC $245M 2026, +5%/yr historical-record 1.5% of revenue; spin removes ~$22M

17.2 Scenario outputs ($M except EPS; 2027+ ex-Mobility)

2026 2027 2028 2029 2030 2031 2032
Revenue — bear 15,195 13,920 14,173 14,677 15,199 15,740 16,302
Revenue — base 15,458 15,241 16,110 17,030 18,004 19,034 20,123
Revenue — bull 15,644 15,785 17,059 18,437 19,860 21,395 23,051
GAAP OP — bear 6,665 5,912 5,977 6,218 6,467 6,723 6,988
GAAP OP — base 6,841 6,936 7,449 7,981 8,547 9,147 9,783
GAAP OP — bull 6,993 7,363 8,189 9,079 9,981 10,960 12,025
FCF — base 5,823 5,778 6,150 6,539 6,957 7,404 7,880
GAAP EPS — bear 15.72 14.14 14.60 15.54 16.55 17.61 18.74
GAAP EPS — base 16.14 16.79 18.56 20.46 22.56 24.84 27.34
GAAP EPS — bull 16.59 18.05 20.80 23.87 27.15 30.83 34.97

The 2027 base-case revenue dip is the Mobility perimeter exit plus the SLB carve-out, not organic decline (organic ex-Mobility 2026→2027 is +5.0%). Ex-Mobility revenue CAGR 2026→2032: bear +2.3%, base +5.6%, bull +7.8%; 2032 GAAP operating margin 42.9% / 48.6% / 52.2%. The bear's 2027 (revenue −8.4%, EPS $14.14) is a milder replay of 2022 — Ratings expenses flex down at the documented ~90% decremental — and EPS regains the 2026 level only in 2029; a full 2022-severity replay would cut deeper (assumption note above). An incremental-margin check caps every segment-year at its historical best (Ratings 105%, Indices 85%, MI adjusted 59.4%, Energy ~57%); no scenario-year exceeds its ceiling.

Reconciliation to the guide and consensus [estimate: bridge arithmetic in the model workpaper]: base 2026 translates to an adjusted, full-Mobility-year EPS of ≈$19.3 — at or just below the $19.40-19.65 guide floor and ~1.6% below the $19.63 consensus — deliberately, because the base takes the ~6% MI print, +5% issuance with the pre-announced negative Q4, and Energy at its cut guide's mid. By 2028 the base sits ~11-12% below consensus on a basis-adjusted comparison, driven by the Indices fee-rate drag, issuance at +4% rather than a re-acceleration, and MI at the print rather than the band.

17.3 Sensitivities (single-driver perturbations, Δ vs base in terminal-2032 operating income)

Driver move Δ2032 OI $M Δ2032 FCF $M
MI subscription +2pp/yr (6→8%) +867 +670
All segment expense growth +1pp/yr −671 −526
Billed issuance +2pp/yr (4→6%) +482 +372
Indices fee compression −6%/yr instead of −4%/yr −244 −188
Average ETF AUM +2pp/yr +239 +185

MI subscription outweighs issuance in terminal-year terms because it compounds on the largest segment; issuance dominates path volatility (the bear's 2027 down-leg alone removes ~$1,024M of that year's operating income). The Indices pair (AUM × rate) together swings ±$480M, a quarter of which is not SPGI's (NCI). Standing flags on the outputs: the Indices fee-rate range is set against its own history (biasing base Indices output high), the Energy base takes management's band floor in the division with the weakest guidance record, the Corporate line embeds a ~$50-115M/yr optimistic bias in all scenarios, and the issuance bull/bear tails are narrower than the realized guide-error distribution — the base case's center survives each of these individually, but the errors share a direction on three of the four.

18. Risks

Ranked by probability within three years × financial exposure × how much is already mitigated. Revenue shares are FY2025.

R1. AI substitution / distribution shift in the data businesses — severe. Mechanism: LLMs plus off-the-shelf models push the cost of processing information toward zero; clients shift consumption from the desktop/workflow layer to in-house LLM channels; data flowing through third-party AI channels leaks or is synthesized ("third parties could also use our data with AI tools to create their own insights and potentially supplant our products and services" — 10-K FY2025). The dependency cuts both ways: SPGI's own AI features rest on third-party models whose providers may discontinue them. Exposure: MI's $4,902M (32% of revenue), of which the desktop is the most substitutable layer (~6% of enterprise revenue per management); the market has already imposed a penalty (§15.4). The 10-K's AI risk language escalated three consecutive years and roughly tripled in FY2025. In the filings, management does not treat the risk as noise, whatever the calls say.▪ Leading indicators: MI ACV growth (Q1 2026 ~6%, a tick down inside an acceleration story), net renewal rates, desktop cancellations, whether the no-training clause survives platform negotiating leverage, and the AI-cohort metrics management chose. Mitigation: license-gated MCP distribution, enterprise pricing rather than seats, and the defensible sub-layers (workflow networks, contributory datasets).

R2. Ratings issuance cyclicality into record compares — severe, cyclical. 52% of Ratings revenue is transaction; the proven drawdown is 2022 (revenue −26%, transaction −45%, margin −9.4pp at a 91% decremental). The current setup: two consecutive record issuance years, a 2026 guide of low-to-mid single digits with a pre-announced negative Q4, and Q1 2026's +14% absorbed as hyperscaler pull-forward — part of the year's budget is pre-spent. Exposure math: a 2022-scale transaction decline from the 2025 base removes roughly $1.1B of revenue and ~$0.7-1.0B of operating profit [estimate]. The correlation warning: the same AI capex cycle that inflates hyperscaler issuance today is the mechanism that would deflate it — an AI-capex bust would trigger R2 and re-price R1 at the same time, while the buyback (R10) will have consumed balance-sheet room at higher prices. Leading indicators: monthly billed issuance and mix, RES revenue (management's stated issuance precursor), hyperscaler bond calendars, HY/IG spreads.

R3. Private credit — growth vector with embedded disintermediation and shopping risk — high. Issuance migrates between public and private with credit conditions (management's own description); smaller agencies are more active in private ratings, where the SEC calls shopping pressure "more acute"; and regulatory attention is building (Moody's FY2025 10-K names it). The 2005-07 structured-finance episode is the precedent for what shopping does to an issuer-pays franchise — S&P paid $1,375M to DOJ/19 states + DC and $125M to CalPERS in 2015 (justice.gov, accessed 2026-07-01 [web]), and an Australian class action over 2005-07 CDO ratings, filed 2020, remains open with no accrual or range disclosed (10-K FY2025). Exposure/opportunity: >$600M enterprise private-markets revenue, Ratings private credit +25% (Q1 2026), against a bank-loan book already shrinking.

R4. Indices sponsor concentration, fee mix-down, self-indexing — high severity, low near-term probability. 65% of segment revenue is asset-linked to sponsor AUM; realized pricing compresses ~6%/yr; the Vanguard-2012 precedent shows what sponsor defection does to an index stock (MSCI −27% in a day). The flagship complex is protected by the derivatives ecosystem and mandate language; second-tier indices are not. Two disclosure changes cut the wrong way: the standalone Indices customer-concentration risk factor was deleted from the FY2024 10-K with no explanation, and no top-sponsor revenue share is disclosed anywhere — the concentration is unsized beyond SPY's ~10% of segment revenue (§20). The CME/CGIS put ($4.9B) sits on top. Leading indicators: realized bp per AUM dollar, share of AUM in sub-5bp products, sponsor benchmark RFPs, SPX ADV.

R5. Spin execution and stranded costs — medium-high, time-boxed. The spin completed 2026-07-01; what remains is the recast (H2 2026), RemainCo stranded costs (unestimated by any available source — management will exclude them from adjusted guidance), the ~$2B SpinCo-debt-funded cash payment that part-funds the 2026 buyback (the completion release describes only the share distribution; the cash mechanics are unconfirmed), and the loss of the most counter-cyclical consumer asset. RemainCo is more capital-markets-geared: Ratings plus Indices rise to ~46% of ex-Mobility revenue.

R6. Single-point operational/technology failure; cloud concentration — medium-high. Benchmark publishers sell reliability. The realized precedent: a May 2024 outage from an erroneous internal code change cut access to "a significant amount" of products for 1-52 hours and revealed "deficiencies in our controls and system architecture" (10-K FY2024) — specifics that were deleted from the FY2025 10-K even as the surrounding risk language grew. AWS is the named critical dependency; attackers target index composition and methodology changes (10-K FY2025) — front-running via breach is an SPGI-specific threat. The LIBOR history shows what an integrity failure does to a submission-based benchmark; IOSCO alignment and AFM/FCA supervision are the mitigation for Platts.

R7. Data-supplier squeeze — medium. Supplier consolidation has already raised fees (realized, past-tense in the FY2024 10-K); suppliers are also competitors; some critical datasets have no alternative source (new FY2025 language). MI absorbs the squeeze on an already-thin GAAP margin.

R8. Regulatory expansion — medium, chronic. NRSRO exams, EU/UK CRA regimes, benchmark regulation (with the EU already intervening in energy-price mechanisms), the EU ESG-ratings regulation (mid-2026) and FCA supervision from 2028, GDPR/DORA/DPPA (the last quantified at $2,500 liquidated damages per individual — vehicle-records exposure that left with the spin). Cost-and-conduct constraints rather than franchise threats; the same regimes are entry barriers.

R9. Energy demand, sanctions, Upstream — medium. Sanctions cut 100-140bp from the core lines' Q1 2026 growth and lap in Q3 2026; the Iran conflict pressured client decision-making; Upstream client consolidation shrinks seats; CERA Titan is an unlaunched fix. Volatility cuts both ways — it lifted GTS ~30% in the same quarter.

R10. Leverage and capital-return appetite under a new CFO — low-medium, watch. $5B repurchased in 2025 with the Q4 tranche partly debt/CP-funded; Q1 2026 buybacks +54% y/y; ≥100% of adjusted FCF committed for 2026 plus the spin dividend. Not a solvency issue at 22.6x interest coverage; it removes slack where the CME put and an issuance downcycle would land together. (The 10-K's "may incur substantial additional indebtedness" language is unchanged since at least FY2022 — the loosening is behavioral, not a disclosure shift.)

The correlated scenario. One macro factor — capital-markets activity — sits under R2, R4, and parts of R1/R9: a credit/equity shock takes Ratings transaction revenue, Indices asset-linked fees, MI's recurring variable line (~$623M of loan-market plumbing), and Energy trading services down together. An AI-capex bust is the worst single scenario because it triggers R2 and re-validates R1 at once. The 2022 precedent shows the diversification working (subscriptions grew through the issuance trough); the post-spin company has less of it.

19. Source disclosure

Databases and stores used. Research archive (241 items: 10 10-Ks FY2016-FY2025, 6 10-Qs, 24 earnings transcripts Q2 2020-Q1 2026, 186 presentation transcripts 2016-2026 including four Investor Days and the merger calls, 15 decks / 878 rendered slides); the legacy Call-extraction store (60 earnings transcripts back to Q2 2011); SEC XBRL companyfacts (SPGI + 18-name reference class + 7 peers); EDGAR press releases (23); DEF 14A proxies 2018-2026 (9, parsed); live Bloomberg terminal via xbbg (prices, BEst consensus with dispersion, earnings history, 30-row comp table — pull 2026-07-01); Daloopa workbook (second source; a pre-2021 period-mapping defect was found and worked around); OC-Sellside SQLite (one document: Goldman Sachs sector report, 2026-04-26); peer filings in archive (MCO, MSCI, FDS, VRSK, ICE, CME, MORN, CBOE, SLB, AMZN); SPY N-30D filings pulled from EDGAR and archived; SEC OCR NRSRO staff reports (Jan-2025 and Apr-2026 editions, archived); the 2026 AGM voting-results 8-K (pulled and archived).

Bootstrapped this run. OC-Call-extraction scaffolding + EDGAR pull; presentation-transcript archive (186 files); 15 Quartr decks; peer filings for MCO/SLB/VRSK/FDS; SPY N-30D; the 18-company base-rate panel (302 company-years); daily price history (Yahoo, rescaled ×1.057 to BBG levels and validated against four ASR VWAPs).

Known gaps and failures (full log in data_inventory.md): the codex guidance-extraction and sell-side digest pipelines are broken (guidance record reconstructed manually); 10K-redlines has no SPGI coverage (Item 1A diffs computed directly from the archive); no SPGI-specific sell-side bear case exists locally and the lone Bloomberg SELL contributor was not identified; Mobility's Form 10 was never pulled (four agents flagged it); IHS Markit's standalone 10-Ks were not pulled; Crisil was sourced from a secondary aggregator; pre-2022 billed issuance dollars do not exist in filings; segment-level consensus was not obtainable; BEST_FCF returns null for SPGI; spglobal.com/usratingsfees, morningstar.com, ceraweek.com, mediadb.eu, rbnenergy.com and fintechfutures.com blocked automated fetch.

Web sources used (domains, approximate counts of distinct material sources): sec.gov (2 regulator reports), esma.europa.eu (1), kbra.com (2), etfstream/etftrends/etfexpress/etfgi (4), tradersmagazine/mondovisione (Burton-Taylor, 2), lseg.com (1), globenewswire/prnewswire/press.spglobal.com (deal and spin releases, ~8), justice.gov (1), wikipedia.org (2), etfstrategy.com (1), capitalone.com (1), edmunds.com (1), alpha-sense.com (1), versana.io (1), stocktitan/quiverquant (Form 4 trackers, 2), marketbeat/stockanalysis (analyst actions, 2), tikr.com (3), morningstar.com (1, unreachable), costbench.com (1), capterra.com (1), newsroom.morningstar.com/crsp.org (2), bloomberg.com (2), money.cnn.com (1), preqin.com (1), experian.com (1), investywise.com (1, Crisil aggregator), uk.globaldatabase.com (1, weak).

Claims flagged [web, unverified] for reader spot-check: (1) the S&P US ratings list price of 8.35 bps / $155K minimum (Jan 2026, +3.1% y/y) and therefore the 69-71% realized-to-list ratio — both the Morningstar summary and S&P's own fee page return HTTP 403; (2) Cap IQ Pro per-contract procurement pricing (median ≈$53K/yr, costbench/Vendr); (3) the Morningstar "oversold" headline (cited as sentiment only); (4) CERAWeek pass pricing (gated); (5) the Mizuho-conference IR quantification (Cap IQ <6% of revenue; benchmarks ~3/4 of profit) — sourced via a tikr.com paraphrase, the primary transcript postdates the archive cutoff; (6) Experian AutoCheck's ~11B record count (page no longer displays it); (7) Cheung's pre-CEO share sales (gurufocus, unverified against Form 4 originals); (8) Argus Media financials (weak third-party estimates).

Estimates by method (the [estimate:] figures that survive into this dossier): ratio/derived-from-disclosures (realized fee rates, take-rates, per-contract royalties, per-seat monetization, capture ratios) ~12; residual/triangulation (non-transaction split, Upstream size, CERAWeek, Fitch revenue, average MI relationship, per-dealer CARFAX) ~8; roll-forward/mix arithmetic (Energy business lines, spin margin mix-lift, exit share of IHSM revenue) ~5; segment-returns constructions (Energy/Mobility/Ratings returns on segment assets, ROIC/WACC yardsticks) ~5; market-data constructions (share-of-industry, HHI, price rescaling) ~6; model bridge arithmetic (guide reconciliation) ~2.

Claim-verification coverage. The adversarial pass verified 150 of 272 eligible claims under tiered sampling — high-confidence locally-cited claims were sampled rather than exhaustively checked, and claims not individually re-checked are used as written with their citations. Of the verified set, the large majority reproduced exactly; the corrections that survived into this dossier are incorporated as substance (the narrowed formulations in §7-§11 and §17). A separate assumption-refutation pass attacked all 27 model assumptions: 20 held, 6 were judged weak (both issuance ranges, the Ratings fee-rate drift, Energy growth, Corporate Unallocated — flagged inline in §17), and one was contradicted by its own history (the Indices fee-rate range — flagged prominently in §17).

External datasets: identified but not fetched this run; the menu and how each would fold in is Appendix C. No fetch failures because no fetches were attempted; the one identified dataset with real analytic cost left on the table is the FRED rate/spread series (§20).

20. What we still don't know / monitoring KPIs

Named gaps, from the completeness review and the workpapers' open questions. The first item governs the whole document.

  1. The ex-Mobility recast baseline does not exist yet. Every consolidated level in this dossier — FY2025 anchors, the 2026 guide, all visible consensus — includes Mobility. SPGI recasts 2025 quarters and re-guides in H2 2026; screens will show phantom growth breaks at the recast, and the modeled Corporate line and the mechanical ~1.2-1.4pp margin mix-lift can only be tested against it then. Monitoring: the recast 8-K/press release; the re-guide.
  2. Competitor AI counter-positioning was not examined. Whether Bloomberg, LSEG-Microsoft, FactSet and Moody's have matched SPGI's MCP/LLM-platform channel decides how much the §3 MI conclusion and the AI-channel wins generalize. Monitoring: rival platform announcements; win/loss commentary; MI ACV vs peers' ARR/ASV growth each quarter.
  3. Is the structured-finance share collapse discipline or defeat? CLO 60.5→47.0%, CMBS 38.1→13.0% in two years; no source distinguishes criteria discipline from lost mandates. Monitoring: S&P's public criteria-change history; SF trade-press league-table commentary; whether RMBS gains continue.
  4. Can the AI renewal-uplift anecdotes be corroborated? The most-quoted bull datapoints (35-45% uplifts on two contracts, the 20%-premium win, +30% relative ACV) are management-selected, n≤2, without cohort controls. Monitoring: whether the metrics reappear each quarter with growing n; any customer-side or procurement-press corroboration.
  5. Indices sponsor concentration beyond SPY is unsized, and the FY2024 10-K deleted the concentration risk factor without explanation. The obvious fund-filings pass (iShares/Vanguard/SSGA annual reports disclose license fees) was named and deferred. Monitoring: that pass; any sponsor RFP or fee-renegotiation news; the realized-rate series.
  6. Mobility Global's Form 10 was never read — the definitive source for CARFAX standalone margins, the D&A composition contradiction, DPPA litigation exposure, and the final ~$2B debt/dividend terms that part-fund SPGI's 2026 buyback. Monitoring: pull MBGL's Form 10/10-Q; confirm the cash payment to SPGI.
  7. No real bear thesis was available. The lone Bloomberg SELL was never identified; §8 argues against a reconstructed bear case. Monitoring: obtain the sell-rated research; watch the short-interest and the marginal seller's argument.
  8. The Ratings pricing anchor is unverified. The 8.35bp/$155K list price rests on an unreachable page; one browser fetch of spglobal.com/usratingsfees converts the pricing-power section from [web, unverified] to primary.
  9. Ratings talent/capacity has no evidence base — headcount is regulator-sourced and dated (Dec 2024), comp is ~73% of ratings cost by proxy, and the FY2025 10-K added an AI-talent risk factor. Monitoring: OCR 2026 report analyst counts; attrition/hiring signals.
  10. Crisil (~9% of Ratings revenue) was sourced second-hand; its own published results were never read and INR translation is unmodeled.
  11. IHS Markit's standalone record (FY2014-FY2021) was never pulled from EDGAR, so the 46-60% exited-revenue estimate rests on web-sourced IHSM figures and Energy/Mobility have a three-year through-cycle history.
  12. The multi-year-drawdown test for Indices and MI rests on 2022 alone (rescued by +38% ETD royalties); 2008-09 asset-linked and Capital IQ behavior sits in pre-2016 10-Ks on EDGAR and would calibrate the flat-market scenario the 10-12% target and the model bear depend on.
  13. Billed issuance before 2022 does not exist as a disclosed dollar series — the trough-to-trough math before 2022 uses revenue proxies, and the deceleration debate is calibrated on one disclosed cycle. Unfillable; stated so the reader weighs §14 accordingly.
  14. RemainCo stranded costs are unestimated — Mobility quantified its side ($20-25M run-rate + SBC step-up); SPGI's side is unestimated, and management excludes them from adjusted guidance, so the 2026-27 margin-expansion story cannot yet be split into operating vs definitional components.
  15. Smaller open items: what happened to the 2018 S&P Global (China) Ratings greenfield (silent since 2018 — the cleanest test of the disappearing-strategy pattern); the FRED rate/spread series identified but unfetched (would give the issuance analysis an independent macro driver — Appendix C); the full NEO/director Form 4 record; the Dow Jones brand license auto-renewal (initial term ended 2026-06-30); the fate of the ≥100%-of-FCF buyback if the stock recovers; MI's net-renewal-rate level; Ratings' private-credit revenue base in dollars; the post-May-2026 event transcripts (the Mizuho fireside is cited via a blog paraphrase).

Standing monitoring dashboard (the five series that decide the open questions): MI organic ACV growth and net renewal rates; monthly billed issuance and its IG/HY/loan mix; the Indices realized asset-linked rate; Energy subscription growth once sanctions lap (Q4 2026); and the AI-monetization metrics management itself selected (Kensho API customers, call volumes, AI-cohort ACV/retention premia).

Appendix A — Investor-presentation insights & key slides

Digest of the deck-only material woven into the body (15 decks, 878 slides, 2014-2026), with the section each informs:

  • The moat quantification and its asymmetry (§3). The enterprise revenue pyramid — >95% proprietary, benchmarks 63% of ex-Mobility revenue — is deck-only (Investor Day 2025, slide 11), as is the MI-level version that undercuts it: 12% undifferentiated plus 23% redistributed Ratings content inside MI (Q1 2026 update, slide 11). Neither decomposition appears in any filing.
  • Ratings' true downside (§1.1, §8 V7). The 2014 Investor Day preserves the pre-filing-archive history: Ratings revenue −26% in 2008, structured finance from 44% of revenue (2007) to 14% (2013), transaction share 49%→30% through the bust — the calibration for any issuance-bust case, and a counterweight to the 2018 deck's "resilience panel" built on the US-corporates sub-segment.
  • The provenance map of the merger (§11). The 2022 completion deck's product-line revenue split by SPGI/INFO origin (Desktop 100% legacy SPGI; Enterprise Solutions 99% INFO; Upstream 100% INFO) exists nowhere else and identifies which businesses were bought — and which of those have since been sold.
  • Deal terms the filings do not repeat (§11). IHS Markit at 28.2x LTM EBITDA pre-synergies / 19.6x post (announcement deck); With Intelligence integration KPIs (>75% of datasets linked in 25 days; 200+ cross-sell leads in 60 days).
  • The target scorecard (§10, §14). Each Investor Day's division target models and their fates — MI's 35-37% margin model missed and silently rebased; Ratings' 58-60% exceeded by ~5pp; the $800M sustainability target at ~48% and deleted; division margin guidance discontinued at ID 2025 after the misses — are reconstructable only from the deck series.
  • Capital-allocation philosophy (§10). "A point of revenue growth creates ~5x more enterprise value than a point of margin" (ID 2018, citing HOLT) — the stated frame behind a growth-priced mega-merger; and the 145%-of-FCF-returned-since-2022 slide (ID 2025) that quantifies the framework/practice gap.
  • Platts depth over time (§3.4). 12,000 daily assessments and >60% of world crude (2014) → 15,000+ and ~70% (2025): the benchmark position deepened over 11 years.
  • Mobility standalone economics (§1.4). The SpinCo carve-out financials, the $1B deferred-tax drag, dis-synergy quantification, and the 2015-2025 above-market growth record (one down year, −2% in 2020) are all from the 2026-05-12 SpinCo Investor Day.
  • Quarterly strategic-KPI series (§13.4). Private Market Solutions revenue ($148M → $169M/quarter through 2025), the sustainability series through its final print ($101M, −3%), and the revenue-synergy run-rate track to $355M are presentation-only series.
  • The China plan that vanished (§9, §20). ID 2018's 100%-owned Chinese rating agency is never mentioned again in any deck or transcript.

Key slides

Appendix B — Web source excerpts

Ordered by first appearance in the body. Each entry: the claim supported, the link, access date, and the relevant passage.

  1. S&P US ratings list price 8.35 bps / $155K minimum (Jan 2026) — morningstar.com/company-reports/1416922, accessed 2026-07-01 [web, unverified]. Search-surfaced summary of S&P's published US fee disclosure: "8.35 basis points with a minimum fee of $155,000 ... a 3.1% increase from the 8.10 basis points list price in January 2025." Direct fetch of both this page and spglobal.com/usratingsfees returned HTTP 403.
  2. SEC OCR staff reports (NRSRO market structure) — sec.gov/files/2025-ocr-staff-report-compliant-4-24-26.pdf (data as of 2024-12-31) and sec.gov/files/jan-2025-ocr-staff-report.pdf (Dec-2023 data), accessed 2026-07-01; archived. Footnote 19 quotes DBRS: "with limited exception, all major indices exclude securities that do not have a credit rating from Fitch, Moody's, or S&P." Charts supply shares (S&P 49.60%), analyst counts (1,725/1,673/1,110), Green Street CLO/CMBS shares, and: rating-shopping pressures are "more acute in the context of private ratings given a lack of transparency to the broader market."
  3. ESMA EU market shares — esma.europa.eu CRA Market Share Report 2024, accessed 2026-07-01. Table p. 8: S&P 48.63%→47.81%, Moody's 31.45%→30.59%, Fitch 10.26%→11.86%.
  4. KBRA private-credit volume — kbra.com Q4 2025 Middle Market Borrower Surveillance Compendium, accessed 2026-07-01. "3,649 middle market corporate credit assessments... 2,416 unique sponsored borrowers," covering more than $1T of private direct-lending debt.
  5. Hearst/Fitch record profit — bloomberg.com, 2026-02-17, accessed 2026-07-01. Headline: "Hearst Posts Record Profit, Citing Strength of Fitch Bond Agency."
  6. Index-industry revenue and shares — etfstream.com, accessed 2026-07-01. "Indexing industry revenue hit [a] record $7.2bn" in 2025, +13.4%; S&P DJI $1.85B, MSCI $1.79B, FTSE Russell $1.29B; "the top three players won more than two-thirds of the indexing industry's revenues."
  7. Vanguard's 2012 benchmark switch — etfstrategy.com, 2012-10-05, accessed 2026-07-01. 22 funds, $537B of assets, to FTSE (6 international, $170B) and CRSP (16 US, $367B) to cut licensing costs; "MSCI shares fell 27%, erasing over $1 billion in market value."
  8. Global ETF industry assets — etfexpress.com, 2026-01-21, accessed 2026-07-01. Record $19.85T at end-2025 with $2.37T of 2025 net inflows (ETFGI data); end-2016 base $3.546T from ETFGI's year-end 2016 reporting.
  9. LSEG / FTSE Russell 2025 results — lseg.com 2025 Preliminary Results, accessed 2026-07-01. FTSE Russell £954M +7.3% organic; asset-based growth "moderated by... the impact of a mandate loss last year"; ETF AUM $1,827B; adjusted EBITDA margin 66.6%.
  10. Nasdaq index business — Nasdaq Q4/FY2025 results, globenewswire.com, accessed 2026-07-01. Index revenue $827M (+17%); Q4 $232M (+23%); record $99B TTM net inflows; ETP AUM $882B.
  11. Burton-Taylor market-data industry sizing — mondovisione.com and tradersmagazine.com, accessed 2026-07-01. 2025 spend a record $49.2B (+6.5%); 2024 $44.3B (+6.4%); "Bloomberg and LSEG... capture one-half of the market's revenues."
  12. AlphaSense scale — alpha-sense.com press release, accessed 2026-07-01. ARR "surpassing $600M" (from $500M in October 2025); $350M raised at a $7.5B valuation; 7,000+ enterprise customers.
  13. Versana consortium — versana.io and [prnewswire funding release], accessed 2026-07-01. $43M raise closed 2026-04-30 led by BNP Paribas; Mizuho "the 14th top-tier financial institution" on the platform; founding members J.P. Morgan and Bank of America; targets golden-source data across "the $9 trillion broadly syndicated loan (BSL) and private credit markets."
  14. Preqin/BlackRock private-markets data pool — asiaasset.com, accessed 2026-07-01. BlackRock completed the $3.2B Preqin acquisition 2025-03-03; sized private-markets data at ~$8B growing ~12%/yr.
  15. Aramco/Argus benchmark switch — en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Argus_Sour_Crude_Index, accessed 2026-07-01. Saudi Aramco adopted ASCI for US-bound crude in 2009, dropping WTI-based pricing; Kuwait (2009) and Iraq (2010) followed.
  16. Enverus scale — [prnewswire, 2023-07-12], accessed 2026-07-01. Enverus "crossed $500 million in annual recurring revenue"; Blackstone's 2025 acquisition at >$6B per contemporaneous coverage.
  17. CARFAX vs AutoCheck pricing — capitalone.com comparison, accessed 2026-07-01 (re-fetched 2026-07-02). CARFAX $44.99 single report; AutoCheck $29.99 single / $59.99 for five on re-fetch (the page showed $24.99 / $49.99-for-25 at first access — pricing apparently changed between fetches).
  18. News Corp energy-information market — News Corp investor communications via trade coverage, accessed 2026-07-01. "$8 billion energy information market projected to grow 8% to 10% annually over the medium term. Dow Jones Energy currently has about 3% share."
  19. DOJ settlement — justice.gov, 2015-02-03, accessed 2026-07-01. "$1.375 Billion Settlement with S&P for Defrauding Investors in the Lead Up to the Financial Crisis"; the separate CalPERS settlement was $125M.
  20. 2011 activist push — money.cnn.com, 2011-09-12, accessed 2026-07-01. Jana Partners (3.3%) and Ontario Teachers' (2.3%) pushed for a breakup; the split was announced 2011-09-12.
  21. IHS Markit merger terms — press.spglobal.com, 2020-11-30, accessed 2026-07-01. "Enterprise value of $44 billion, including $4.8 billion of net debt"; ~4.7% premium per contemporaneous coverage; IHSM FY2021 revenue $4,659M from its own results releases.
  22. With Intelligence terms — [prnewswire release 302584783], accessed 2026-07-01. "$130 million of expected additional revenue in 2025 with organic annual contract value growth in the high teens" on the $1.8B purchase.
  23. Mobility spin completion — prnewswire, 2026-07-01, accessed 2026-07-01. Separation of Mobility Global Inc. completed "effective as of 12:01 a.m. New York City time on July 1, 2026"; one MBGL share per SPGI share; regular-way NYSE trading began 2026-07-01.
  24. CEO open-market purchase — stocktitan.net Form 4 report, accessed 2026-07-01. Cheung bought 2,322 shares at a weighted average $429.93 on 2026-04-29, lifting direct holdings to 27,518 — consistent with the proxy table plus the purchase.
  25. Analyst actions and targets — marketbeat.com, accessed 2026-07-01. The per-firm action table in §14/§15 context (e.g., Stifel 599→489 on 2026-02-11; Goldman 539→490 on 2026-07-01); Bloomberg's broader contributor set (29 analysts: 26 buy / 2 hold / 1 sell, mean target $502.18) is the primary.
  26. IR quantification of AI exposure — tikr.com blog, 2026-06-11, accessed 2026-07-01 [web, unverified]. Paraphrasing the 2026-06-10 Mizuho conference: Cap IQ is "less than 6% of our total revenue and an even smaller percentage of our profits"; ~2/3 of revenue and ~3/4 of profit from benchmark businesses.
  27. Bloomberg LP scale — [Wikipedia/Bloomberg press coverage], accessed 2026-07-01. >355,000 terminal subscribers (May 2025); revenue estimates $13.3B (2024, press) to ~$15B (Wikipedia infobox) — the conservative figure is used throughout.
  28. Cboe SPX volumes — [cboe.com State of the Options Industry 2025], accessed 2026-07-01. Record 3.9M SPX options ADV in 2025, 59% 0DTE.

Appendix C — External data sources

This appendix triangulates the body's segment analysis against third-party data. Each S&P Global revenue driver is checked with an independent external series, so the recovery, growth, and share claims elsewhere in the dossier rest on more than management's own disclosure. Six datasets are pulled by coded connectors and persisted as tidy CSVs under archive/SPGI/edge/ (marked ✓, values reconciled against the filings); twelve are harvested from primary documents (SIFMA Fact Book, SEC workbooks, ESMA and S&P DJI publications) and carry the confidence of their source. These are proxies for the drivers, not S&P's books; the point is directional corroboration and the size of the gap between the external signal and the reported result.

C.1 Ratings — the issuance fee and the surveillance annuity

Ratings revenue is billed issuance x realized fee rate, plus a recurring surveillance annuity on the stock of outstanding ratings. The external drivers follow directly: issuance volume (how many new deals get rated), the price of credit (spreads determine whether issuers come to market at all), and the count of outstanding ratings (the annuity base). Issuance volumes by asset class, gross, $B:

Asset class 2022 2023 2024 2025
Corporate bonds 1,404 1,508 1,969 ~2,216
Municipal 392 386 514 —
ABS / structured 303 271 388 —
Total long-term FI 8,865 8,282 10,433 —

[data: sifma_issuance (bulk, SIFMA Fact Book p.42; 2025 corporate = estimate) · fred_series ✓]

Chart C.1 — US fee-generating issuance by asset class ($B). [data: sifma_issuance]

Fee-generating issuance troughed in 2022–23 as rates spiked, then reflated in 2024: corporate +31%, municipal +33%, ABS +43%, total long-term issuance +26%. S&P reported +31% Ratings revenue in FY2024 off the same window. The near-match is the useful part. It says the Ratings rebound was driven by volume returning, not by price or mix flattering the number, which is what management claimed and what an outside series now independently confirms. Volume depends on the price of credit: investment-grade spreads averaged 0.86% and high-yield 3.05% in 2025 (near cycle-tights), the condition under which issuers refinance early and pull supply forward. 2025 issuance kept growing but decelerated (corporate ~+13%), consistent with a normalizing, not re-accelerating, transaction line.

The other half of the segment is the annuity, and the external data sizes both its base and S&P's grip on it:

Metric Value
S&P ratings outstanding / share 1.078M / 49.2%
Moody's / Fitch share 31.2% / 12.5%
S&P vs Moody's, non-government 161k vs 143k
S&P Ratings revenue, FY24 $4.4B (+31%) vs Moody's $3.8B
S&P credit analysts 1,725 (the largest analyst base of the Big Three)
S&P EU revenue share 50.4% (2025, up from 47.8%)
US corporate debt outstanding $8.73T (+15% over 5yr)

[data: sec_ocr_nrsro (bulk, SEC NRSRO workbook + 2025 OCR Staff Report) · esma_cra_stats · fred_series ✓]

S&P is the largest ratings franchise on every economic measure: outstanding ratings (49.2% of the US total), revenue ($4.4B, above Moody's $3.8B), analyst base (1,725, the most of any NRSRO), and now a majority of EU rating-activity revenue (50.4%, and the only Big Three member to gain share in 2025). Two qualifications matter for how durable that lead is. First, the headline count is inflated by US public finance: 917k of S&P's 1.08M outstanding ratings are government securities, and on non-government ratings the gap to Moody's narrows to 161k versus 143k, closer to a duopoly than a dominant lead. Second, the strength is uneven by product. S&P leads corporate, sovereign, insurance, and (until 2024) CLOs, but is weak in CMBS (~17% share) and RMBS (~18%), where Fitch, KBRA and DBRS rate most of the deals. The implication is that the moat is scale plus regulatory incumbency concentrated in the plain-vanilla corporate and public-finance categories, and the recurring $8.73T-and-growing debt-outstanding base is what made the annuity half compound through the transaction line's worst years.

C.2 Market Intelligence — the customer base and the private-markets pool

MI sells enterprise subscriptions to banks and asset managers, so its demand is a function of how healthy and how busy those customers are, and how fast the asset pools its workflow tools serve (iLevel and Capital IQ private markets; ClearPar and Wall Street Office in loans) are growing.

Demand proxy Value / trend
Securities-industry employment 1,158k (+20% over 5yr)
Private-fund gross assets $26.9T (+10.5% YoY)
Secondary loan trading volume $971B (2025 record, +18%)
Data-peer revenue, FY25 MSCI $3.1B · Morningstar $2.4B · FactSet $2.3B
Kensho developer adoption kfinance ~17k/mo PyPI, ~14 GitHub stars

[data: bls_employment ✓ · sec_private_fund_stats · lsta_loan_volume · sec_xbrl_concept ✓ · developer_ecosystem_adoption]

Every demand proxy points the same way. Securities-industry payrolls, the seat base for Capital IQ Pro, are up 20% over five years. Private-fund gross assets reached $26.9T and are accelerating into low-double-digit annual growth, the pool that iLevel and Capital IQ's private-markets content sell into (and that BlackRock's Preqin and MSCI's Burgiss now contest). Secondary loan trading set a record $971B in 2025, a direct proxy for the settlement volume ClearPar and Wall Street Office process. On scale, MI's ~$4.9B segment alone is larger than any single data peer (see C.5). The cautions are specific and worth keeping. Kensho's developer footprint is thin, ~14 GitHub stars and ~17k monthly PyPI downloads on its flagship kfinance client, which is a real check on the "LLM-ready API" growth narrative rather than a confirmation of it. Search interest is not usable here: for seat-licensed B2B terminals usage sits behind logins, not public search, so that proxy is directional at best and is flagged accordingly, not charted.

C.3 Indices — the asset-linked base and the derivatives royalty

Indices earns asset-linked fees (basis points on AUM tracking S&P indices) plus exchange-traded-derivative royalties (a per-contract cut of S&P-linked futures and options). The drivers are the AUM in S&P-tracking products, the index level that revalues it, and the volume of S&P-linked derivatives.

Driver 2021 2024 / 25
Assets indexed to the S&P 500 ~$7.1T ~$13.0T (2024)
All S&P DJI, indexed+benchmarked — $27.7T (2024)
US ETF total assets $7.2T $13.4T (2025)
US ETF net issuance $935B ~$1.5T (2025 record)
Index share of US fund assets — 53.8% (majority)
S&P 500 level (avg) / VIX — 6,217 (+14.5%) / 18.9
E-mini S&P 500 open interest — ~1.98M contracts

[data: sp_dji_indexed_assets · ici_fund_flows · sp500_etf_aum · fred_series ✓ · cftc_futures_oi ✓]

Chart C.3 — the asset-linked fee base, 2020–2025. [data: ici_fund_flows, sp_dji_indexed_assets]

The fee base roughly doubled. Assets indexed to the S&P 500 went from about $7T in 2021 to about $13T in 2024, and US ETF assets from $7.2T to $13.4T, with a record ~$1.5T of net inflows in 2025. The mechanism behind the doubling has two parts: AUM equals units held times index level, and both rose (the index compounded +93% over five years while the passive share of US long-term fund assets crossed into the majority at 53.8%). The three largest S&P 500 ETFs alone hold ~$2.65T. The implication is the one tension in the segment. Asset-linked revenue has grown far slower than this AUM base because the blended fee rate is compressing (the body puts it near 2.5bp, down roughly 6%/yr), so the external AUM growth shown here is precisely what offsets the price decline, and the segment's health depends on the volume line staying ahead of the rate line. The derivatives royalty is the complement: E-mini S&P 500 open interest near 1.98M contracts underpins the ETD line that grows on volatility, the piece that rose even in down-equity years.

C.4 Commodity Insights — the Platts benchmark royalty

Platts price assessments are licensed IP, and the royalty scales with the volume of derivatives that cash-settle against them, not with the level of the underlying commodity. So the right proxy is cleared contract volume, led by the LNG (JKM) and Dated Brent benchmarks.

Driver Value / trend
JKM LNG (Platts) futures 1.32M lots (2025, +37%, 4th straight record)
ICE share of JKM ~97% (CME's Platts contracts are thin, OI ~530)
WTI / Brent / Henry Hub, 2025 avg $65.4 / $69.1 / $3.52
Rig count — worldwide / North America ~1,734 (+10% YoY) / 770 (rising)

[data: cme_ice_platts_volume · fred_series ✓ · eia_series ✓ · bakerhughes_rigcount]

Chart C.4 — Platts JKM LNG futures volume (thousand lots). [data: cme_ice_platts_volume]

The clearest growth signal in the whole external set is the JKM LNG contract: 1.32M lots in 2025, up 37%, a fourth consecutive annual record, compounding at 30–50% a year. The critical detail for reading it is where the volume clears. About 97% of JKM open interest is on ICE; CME's own Platts-branded contracts are negligible (Dated Brent open interest around 530). So S&P's LNG-benchmark royalty upside tracks ICE volume, and the CME pages that are easiest to pull are the wrong place to look for it. Spot prices (WTI $65, Brent $69, Henry Hub $3.52 on 2025 averages) are off the 2022 peak and recovering into 2026, and the worldwide rig count is up ~10%, which supports demand for the upstream analytics S&P sells. The implication is that Commodity Insights rides secular LNG and energy-transition data demand far more than the spot cycle: the structural volume growth in benchmarks like JKM is the durable driver, and price is the noise around it.

C.5 Cross-cutting — peer scale

The one comparison that spans the franchises is total revenue against the credit-ratings peer (Moody's) and the pure-play data vendors (MSCI, Morningstar, FactSet), pulled from XBRL and reconciled to the filings.

Company FY2025 revenue vs SPGI
S&P Global $15,336M —
Moody's $7,718M 0.50x
MSCI $3,134M 0.20x
Morningstar $2,446M 0.16x
FactSet $2,322M 0.15x

[data: sec_xbrl_concept ✓]

Chart C.5 — FY2025 revenue, S&P Global vs financial-data peers ($B). [data: sec_xbrl_concept]

S&P Global is roughly twice Moody's and five to seven times any single pure-play data vendor. That scale is the point of the multi-franchise model: no competitor faces S&P across ratings, indices, market data, and commodity benchmarks at once, and each of the peers above competes with only one of its segments. (One caution on this dataset: multi-year growth rates computed from XBRL are unreliable for some filers because the revenue tag was adopted mid-history, so the levels above are exact and tie to the filings, but a CAGR taken straight off the series is not.)

Method and reproducibility. The six ✓ datasets are pulled by coded connectors (fred_series, treasury_auctions, sec_xbrl_concept, bls_employment, eia_series, cftc_futures_oi) and re-run on demand; the twelve harvested datasets are stored as tidy CSVs alongside them. All eighteen live in archive/SPGI/edge/ with per-row source, pull date, method, and confidence, so every figure in this appendix is traceable to a stored series rather than transcribed by hand.

Appendix D — Follow-up Q&A

Follow-up questions researched 2026-07-02 to the same standard as the main dossier — internal sources (filings, transcripts, the dossier workpapers) plus external sources, adversarially verified, descriptive only with no valuation view. The full answers, with citations, live on the companion page appendix_d_followup.html:

  1. SPGI Ratings vs Moody's (MIS): how the two franchises differ — fixed-income category mix, transaction vs recurring, and 10-year growth and margins
  2. Capital IQ Pro vs Bloomberg, FactSet and LSEG/Refinitiv: relative size (revenue, seats, headcount), growth, and exposure to AI-driven disruption
  3. S&P Global Ratings growth: new issuance vs refinancing, how predictive the debt maturity walls are, and how the recurring vs non-recurring customer bases differ

Evidence
